title: Systemic Non-Compliance in Pilot Boarding Arrangements
description:
This theme is characterised by a persistent and widespread failure to rig, maintain, and inspect pilot boarding arrangements in accordance with SOLAS requirements. Across the incident population, deficiencies range from frayed side ropes and improperly placed ladder weights to non-compliant fastening methods, absent securing points, and deteriorated accommodation ladder equipment. What unifies these incidents is not isolated or opportunistic error, but a systemic normalisation of deviance within shipboard safety culture, whereby sub-standard pilot transfer arrangements were repeatedly prepared and used without detection or challenge. The high volume of 'other' category incidents alongside crew injuries underscores that these failures represent a standing operational risk rather than exceptional occurrences, and the breadth of vessel types affected—container ships, bulk carriers, tankers, cruise ships, and general cargo vessels—confirms this is an industry-wide rather than sector-specific problem.

The latent causal architecture of this theme involves the convergence of hardware deterioration, procedural breakdown, and human factors deficiencies. On the hardware side, pilot ladders and accommodation ladders were permitted to degrade through absent or ineffective maintenance regimes, with no mechanism to trigger timely inspection or replacement before operational deployment. Procedurally, pre-arrival checklists either did not exist, were not enforced, or lacked sufficient specificity to capture SOLAS rigging non-conformances, allowing deficient arrangements to pass unchallenged. Human factors analysis points to crew who lacked adequate familiarisation with SOLAS pilot transfer requirements, combined with insufficient officer oversight of a safety-critical evolution. Night-time operating conditions further compounded these weaknesses by reducing the visibility available during rigging and inspection, increasing the probability that physical defects would be overlooked. Collectively, these factors indicate a failure of the vessel's Safety Management System to treat pilot boarding arrangements as a standing regulatory compliance requirement subject to rigorous pre-operation verification.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulation 23: Pilot Transfer Arrangements)
SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code)
SOLAS Chapter I – General Provisions (Surveys, Certification, and Port State Control)
preventable_by: Mandatory implementation of a dedicated pre-arrival pilot boarding equipment checklist, aligned explicitly with SOLAS Regulation V/23 and IMO Resolution A.1045(27), requiring a named responsible officer to physically inspect and certify ladder condition, rigging method, fastening compliance, and stanchion geometry before every pilot embarkation, with completed records retained for Port State Control review.

Establishment of a planned maintenance system (PMS) entry specifically for pilot ladders and associated boarding equipment, prescribing defined inspection intervals, condition criteria for withdrawal from service, and mandatory replacement schedules for side ropes, steps, and securing hardware, with defect rectification tracked to closure and verified by the Master prior to the next port arrival.

Delivery of targeted crew familiarisation and competency verification training on SOLAS-compliant pilot transfer arrangements for all officers and ratings involved in rigging operations, including practical demonstrations of correct fastening methods, prohibited configurations, and night-time inspection protocols, with refresher training incorporated into vessel safety management routines and records maintained as part of the SMS audit trail.

representative_cases.description: Container ship's pilot ladder not rigged per SOLAS regulations. Weight of the ladder was resting on a step. The side ropes of the ladder were very frayed in places. The stanchions did not go all the way down to the deck and were too far apart.

Bulk carrier's pilot ladder not rigged per SOLAS regulations. The ladder was shackled to the deck and not backed up. Accommodation ladder had no inboard station and all the gear was in poor condition.

A general cargo vessel had a non-SOLAS compliant pilot ladder due to the fact that it was secured with shackles. The pilot ladder arrangement was rectified upon boarding and the master was advised by the pilot. The vessel was reportedly very cooperative.

A cruise ship's pilot ladder did not conform to SOLAS regulations. The pilot ladder was secured to the deck head in the shell door opening instead of the deck. After inspection it seems there were no securing points on the deck.

A crude oil tanker had a non-SOLAS compliant pilot ladder due to the fact that the ladder was not rigged as per the current SOLAS regulations. The level of the pilot slant platform was 2.5m above the waterline and there was a chock missing from a step meaning that the step was un-even/not horizontal.

A container ship's pilot ladder did not conform to SOLAS regulations. Stanchions secured by pins at the base but not rigidly secured at the top such that they had significant give and movement in them. General concerns about diameter of stanchions being too thick, width apart being too great and distance inboard from deck edge also too much.

A bulk carrier had a non-SOLAS compliant pilot ladder due to the fact that it was in poor condition and poor manufacturing standards. The ladder was new however the chocks for the steps were loose meaning that the steps were able to move and one of the spreaders lay flat on the deck when the ladder was laid out on deck, demonstrating how loose it was.

A cruise ship crewmember was climbing down from their bunk when the hook for the ladder broke, causing them to fall to the deck below and fracture a limb. The cause of the accident was attributed to the hook of the ladder broken, which was later repaired. The crew member signed off due to their injury.

A ro-ro cargo vessel had an inadequate pilot ladder arrangement due to the fact that the deck stanchions were not continuous down to the deck to allow the pilot to easily transition from the deck to the ladder side ropes.

Container vessel's pilot ladder not rigged per SOLAS regulations. Ladder was in general condition poor. Three out of four spreaders bars cut less than standard width causing significant rotation away from ships side, when descending last three metres.

title: Systemic Pilot Transfer Arrangement Non-Compliance Across Fleet Operations
description:
This theme is characterised by pervasive and recurring non-compliance with SOLAS pilot transfer arrangement requirements across a broad range of vessel types, including container ships, chemical tankers, oil tankers, and ro-ro vessels. Deficiencies span improper rigging methods, degraded or counterfeit equipment, missing structural components such as stanchions and heaving lines, and failure to cross-reference vessel loading conditions with freeboard height thresholds before deployment. Critically, these are not isolated one-off errors; they represent systemic failures in onboard Safety Management System enforcement, planned maintenance culture, and crew training. The normalisation of non-compliant practices, compounded by underreporting of near-miss events, has allowed these deficiencies to persist across global fleets despite well-established regulatory frameworks and repeated identification through Port State Control detentions and marine safety investigations.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including worn side ropes, missing rubber steps, twisted spreaders, improperly seized rope joints, and the infiltration of counterfeit safety equipment into supply chains — create structural hazards that are frequently undetected due to inadequate inspection regimes and poor equipment status communication. Human factors including insufficient crew familiarity with SOLAS Regulation V/23 technical requirements, absence of verified pre-rigging checklists, lack of supervisory oversight during ladder deployment, and reactive rather than proactive safety cultures further compound the risk. Environmental conditions, particularly night-time operations with reduced visibility, consistently elevate the consequence severity of existing deficiencies by impairing the ability of both crew and pilots to visually identify non-compliant or structurally compromised arrangements during the most safety-critical phase of the boarding or disembarkation operation. The convergence of these latent hardware, human, and environmental factors produces conditions in which a fatal fall-from-height or man-overboard event remains a foreseeable and preventable outcome.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V, Regulation 23 – Pilot Transfer Arrangements
SOLAS Chapter I, Regulation 14 – Maintenance of the Ship and Equipment
SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code)
preventable_by: Mandate the integration of a standardised, SOLAS V/23-referenced pilot transfer arrangement checklist into pre-arrival procedures, requiring documented sign-off by a responsible officer that confirms ladder condition, rigging configuration, and freeboard height compliance against current loading condition data before deployment — with results logged in the vessel's SMS records.

Establish a mandatory fleet-wide competency verification and refresher training programme specifically addressing SOLAS Regulation V/23 technical requirements, including correct rigging methods, prohibited attachment hardware, step spacing tolerances, and tripping line positioning, ensuring all officers responsible for pilot transfer arrangements can demonstrate practical compliance knowledge at scheduled intervals.

Require flag States and Port State Control authorities to enforce pre-arrival pilot transfer equipment certification checks, including third-party procurement auditing to detect counterfeit or substandard ladder components, supported by a structured out-of-service tagging and replacement protocol within the vessel's Planned Maintenance System to eliminate the use of degraded or non-compliant equipment.

representative_cases.description: Container ship's pilot ladder did not conform to SOLAS regulations. The ship is in ballast condition.
A chemical tanker had an inadequate pilot ladder arrangement. There were no stanchions present and the gate and handle were not connected to the deck and were difficult for the pilot to hold onto.
Removable platform at pilot door rigged at heigh less that 5m above sea level, what makes it not compliant.   Master advised to not use the platform in that loading condition.
Construction did not meet current SOLAS standards. Side ropes were two separate lengths seized together underneath the bottom step.
A general cargo vessel's pilot ladder did not conform to SOLAS regulations. A tripping line was rigged between the last two steps.
Ladder secured by shackles. Ladder was not rigged per current SOLAS regulations.
A container vessel had an inadequate pilot ladder because it was supported using a deck tongue and the tripping line was incorrectly rigged.
Construction did not meet current SOLAS standards. Heaving line was not available for immediate use. No stantions on the deck and the deck rail fittings at the entrance are too wide to grip
A ro-ro cargo vessel's pilot ladder was not rigged to SOLAS regulations. The ladder rigged with the step on the holding platform. Master and crew informed and promised it would be changed.
Container ship's ladder was not rigged per current SOLAS regulations. Choke shackles used, vessel in poor condition.

title: COLREGs Non-Compliance and Close-Quarters Collision Risk
description:
This theme is characterised by repeated failures among recreational and small commercial vessel operators to fulfil their obligations under the International Regulations for Preventing Collisions at Sea (COLREGs), particularly with respect to maintaining a proper lookout (Rule 5), proceeding at a safe speed (Rule 6), taking early and substantial action to avoid collision (Rule 8), and adhering to give-way responsibilities (Rules 12 and 16). The incidents share a common pattern: a give-way vessel — typically a sailing craft, personal watercraft, or small recreational powerboat — fails to yield, often due to operator inexperience, inattention, or inadequate watchkeeping routines. This forces the stand-on vessel into reactive, uncoordinated manoeuvres that themselves introduce collision risk. The recurrence of these incidents reflects a systemic deficit in recreational operator competency and compliance culture, rather than isolated individual errors.

Latent human factors dominate this theme, including insufficient training, poor situational awareness, and the absence of enforced night watch routines, as evidenced by obscured cockpits, invisible crew, and erratic helm behaviour. Physical hardware deficiencies — notably cockpit canopies obstructing both outward visibility and crew observability — compound these human failures by degrading the effectiveness of any lookout that is attempted. Environmental conditions, particularly night-time operation and moderate sea states, consistently compress the time available for threat detection and response, reducing the margin for error to near zero. The near-total absence of inter-vessel VHF communication across these incidents means that intentions are never coordinated, leaving collision avoidance dependent on last-second reactive manoeuvres. Together, these factors create a systemic vulnerability concentrated at the intersection of recreational vessel operations, confined or trafficked waters, and reduced-visibility conditions.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulations 19 and 34: navigational watchkeeping, voyage planning, and use of navigational aids)
SOLAS Chapter VIII – Watchkeeping (Standards for watchkeeping at sea, including lookout requirements and master's standing orders)
SOLAS Chapter VI / STCW Code (A-VIII/2) – Principles of Watchkeeping and Collision Avoidance (officer and operator competency in applying COLREGs)
preventable_by: Mandate structured COLREGs competency assessments and practical collision-avoidance training as a condition of recreational vessel licensing, with specific modules on give-way obligations at night, in confined waters, and in mixed-traffic environments — ensuring operators can correctly identify their role and act decisively before close-quarters situations develop.

Require that all vessels operating in trafficked or confined waters at night maintain a continuous VHF bridge-to-bridge watch on Channel 16, and introduce harbour authority or coastguard-led radio communication protocols for small recreational craft transiting busy channels, reducing reliance on sound signals or shouting as last-resort warnings.

Implement and enforce speed restriction zones in near-quay, channel, and high-traffic recreational areas through physical signage, chart notation, and where feasible, automated vessel monitoring — supported by flag state or port authority guidance that explicitly requires operators to reduce to a safe speed consistent with COLREGs Rule 6 when visibility is reduced or traffic density is elevated.

description:

{
  "title": "Night Berthing Contact Incidents: Spatial Awareness and Control Failures",
  "description": "This theme is characterised by a high frequency of low-to-moderate severity contact incidents occurring predominantly during night-time berthing, unberthing, and port manoeuvring operations. The overwhelming dominance of contact incidents (94 of 99 categorised events) underscores a systemic vulnerability in the final approach and alongside phases of port operations, where precise spatial judgement, vessel momentum control, and coordination between bridge teams, pilots, and shore personnel are most critically tested. Common threads across the cluster include degraded visual depth perception under artificial or insufficient night lighting, underestimation of three-dimensional vessel geometry (bow flare, superstructure overhangs, stern knuckle points), inadequate pre-berthing hazard identification, and a recurring disconnect between reported sea states and actual wave heights — a data quality issue that may be masking the true contribution of environmental forces to these events. The incidents span a broad range of vessel types, including ro-ro ferries, cargo vessels, tankers, and offshore support vessels, indicating that the risk is not vessel-type-specific but rather reflects industry-wide procedural and cultural deficiencies in night manoeuvring risk management.\n\nThe latent causal architecture of this theme reflects an interplay of human factors, procedural gaps, and environmental conditions rather than discrete equipment failures. Human factors dominate: over-reliance on experience in lieu of structured risk assessment, insufficient bridge resource management and challenge culture, and poor synchronisation between bridge teams and shore parties during dynamic manoeuvring phases are consistently evident. Procedural deficiencies compound these individual failures — absent or inconsistently applied night berthing checklists, lack of formalised go/no-go criteria for night operations in moderate weather, inadequate pilot-master information exchange, and the absence of explicit abort-and-return contingency procedures all represent latent systemic weaknesses. Environmental conditions, while rarely the sole cause, act as risk amplifiers: moderate wind, residual swell, and reduced visibility narrow the safe operating margin and expose underlying procedural and human factor vulnerabilities. The inaccurate sea state reporting identified across multiple incidents further suggests that environmental risk is being systematically underweighted in pre-manoeuvre assessments, reducing the likelihood of appropriate mitigation such as tug support or deferred operations.",

representative_cases.description: Cargo ship was shifting in dock when it made contact with 3 berthed tugs however no damage was reported.
A tanker was berthing when it made contact with an inner fender with the stern of the vessel.  No reported hull damage other then superficial paint damage. No observed damage to fenders or concrete.
A general cargo vessel was going alongside at a berth when the flare of the bow made contact with a shore-side crane.
A container ship under pilotage was preparing to berth when the bridge wing canopy made contact with a shoreside crane.
General cargo hit a jetty during a swing. The vessel sustained a dent on the port quarter 1m below mid deck. No damage has been reported on the jetty.
A general cargo vessel collided with a buoy whilst departing a port, without the pilot on board. This resulted in damage to the buoy which lost its top mark. The vessel did not report any damage.
A platform supply vessel made contact with a pier whilst entering a port. There were no injuries, pollution or damages reported.
A ro-ro vessel made contact with the knuckle of a quayside during manoeuvring. This resulted in a damaged fender on the shore side.
An offshore support vessel was berthing alongside another offshore support vessel in a port when a protruding piece of metal from the other vessel touched a window and broke it.
Cargo vessel was approaching the berth when it made contact with the quay. This resulted in a cracked quay and the vessel's bow quarter has scratches.

title: Manual Handling Injuries Driven by Procedural and Human Failures
description:
This theme encompasses crew injuries arising from manual handling tasks and physically demanding activities in which latent procedural deficiencies — rather than discrete mechanical failures — are the primary enablers of harm. The incidents are characterised by a convergence of inadequate return-to-work protocols, absent or unenforced manual handling risk assessments, and systemic rest hour non-compliance that collectively leave crew members in a state of unrecognised physical vulnerability. Across these events, self-assessment routinely substitutes for formal medical clearance, ergonomic hazards go unaddressed at the task-design level, and time pressure — whether self-imposed through rushing or externally imposed through emergency tasking — overrides whatever informal precautions crew members might otherwise take. The recurring nature of these incidents reflects not individual lapses in isolation, but an organisational tolerance for procedural gaps that renders foreseeable injuries effectively inevitable.

Latent hardware and equipment design deficiencies — such as poorly accessible dipstick locations requiring dangerous reach postures, and below-deck spaces inadequately configured for safe immersion suit donning — create physical conditions in which even routine tasks carry disproportionate injury risk. These are compounded by human factors including cumulative fatigue from chronic rest hour violations, inexperience among first-contract crew members unfamiliar with vessel-specific spatial hazards, and cognitive underestimation of residual physical vulnerability following prior injury. Environmental conditions, particularly night-time operations, further erode situational awareness and motor precision at precisely the moments when procedural safeguards are most needed. The absence of formal safe work procedures, ergonomic task assessments, and enforced fitness-for-task evaluations means there is no systemic barrier between these converging risk factors and the crew member's body, making injury the predictable outcome of an otherwise manageable set of circumstances.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (equipment accessibility, immersion suit donning procedures, and emergency preparedness drills)
SOLAS Chapter XIII – Verification of Compliance (flag State oversight of rest hour regulations, safe manning, and occupational safety management system enforcement)
SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code requirements for risk assessment, safe work procedures, and crew health and fitness management)
preventable_by: Implement and enforce a formal return-to-work and fitness-for-task medical clearance protocol requiring independent medical sign-off — not crew self-assessment — before any crew member resumes manual handling duties following a musculoskeletal injury, with the protocol embedded in the vessel's SMS and auditable by the flag State.

Conduct task-level ergonomic and manual handling risk assessments for all routine shipboard physical tasks, including storeroom operations, engine room instrumentation access, and cargo handling, with documented controls such as mechanical aids, buddy systems, or equipment repositioning where awkward postures or heavy loads are identified; review assessments whenever equipment layout changes or an incident occurs.

Establish a robust, actively monitored rest hour compliance system with independent verification by the master and company safety management, including mandatory rest day enforcement and a crew fatigue reporting mechanism, ensuring that cumulative fatigue — particularly for crew nearing contract end or new to the vessel — is identified and mitigated before it contributes to injury during routine or emergency tasks.

The crew member had previously injured their shoulder but thought it had heeled. Despite using the correct manual lifting technique on the 20l drum, the crewmember pulled their shoulder. This resulted in a torn rotator cuff.

A cruise ship crew member was walking through the restaurant area when they took a miss-step and twisted their right foot resulting in a fracture. The crew member was carrying a light tray of empty sugar bowls and they twisted their foot at an 'abnormal' angle. The crew member had been working for approximately 5 hours before the incident and was only 10 minutes from the end of their shift. They h

A service ship crewmember was leaning over some railings to reach a dip-stick when they injured their ribs. The crewmember said that they were rushing to get the job done. It was not determined if the ribs were bruised or broken.

A search and rescue vessel was responding to an emergency tasking and was underway when a crewmember went down below to don their immersion suit. The weather was calm at the time, but whilst dressing, the crewmember moved in an awkward manner resulting in a dislocated knee. The knee popped back into place shortly after and the crewmember attended hospital where they were given a knee brace and cru

Passenger ship's crew member was assigned to remove the boxes of different kinds of fruit/vegetables from pallets and arrange them. They were doing this for approximately 3 hours. The following day, they stated that there was pain in their shoulder. No defects found, the crew member has been kept more than 72h off work.

A tug crew member was carrying out maintenance on the tow hook on the aft deck. After greasing the hook, the crew member then attempted to move the tow hook from side to side to make sure there was free movement. The crew member strained their lower back resulting in severe pain and time off work.

During routine maintenance of the IRB, a male vessel crew member lost his footing and fell against railings while transferring from the IRB to the vessel. As a result of the impact he was in pain, however, after a few minutes, the pain subsided and he was able to continue with his normal duties until two days later when he had to be replaced onboard. He was diagnosed with a sprained abdomen

A cruise ship crewmember strained their back whilst cleaning. The crewmember was kneeling down and cleaning a drainage area with a towel. Whilst getting down to floor level, they felt a 'twinge' in their lower back. Initially, the level of pain was low, however the crewmember went to their cabin and later in the day, found that the pain had increased significantly. The area where the person was wo

A ro-ro cargo vessel crew member sustained an injury to their finger. Following radio medical advice that the crew member sought medical care in a suitable timeframe the vessel continued on it's passage.
A cruise ship crewmember broke their ankle. Then they had an allergic reaction to anti-inflammatories administered on board and were evacuated ashore for further treatment.

A fishing vessel crew member sustained an injury while shooting gear. They caught their left hand between the chain and wire. Evacuation by a helicopter was impossible due to weather conditions therefore, the individual was taken to the nearest port to seek further medical assistance.

title: Unknown Theme
description:
```json
{
  "title": "Propeller Fouling and Entanglement During Night Operations",
  "description": "This theme is characterised by a high frequency of propulsion loss events arising from propeller or thruster entanglement with fishing gear, ropes, nets, mooring lines, and surface debris, predominantly occurring during night-time operations across fishing, recreational, and survey vessel categories. The overwhelming majority of incidents result in complete loss of propulsion and subsequent dependency on SAR or peer-vessel towing, elevating routine operational hazards into formal search and rescue events. A consistent pattern emerges whereby reduced ambient lighting during night operations critically impairs the ability of watchkeepers to visually detect low-profile surface obstructions such as pot marker buoys, net floats, and loose lines in sufficient time to execute evasive manoeuvres. The prevalence of this pattern across vessel types and operational contexts indicates a systemic and sector-wide vulnerability rather than isolated human error, with particular concentration among small commercial fishing vessels operating in inshore and coastal waters where static and mobile gear conflicts are frequent.\n\nFrom a latent causation perspective, the incidents collectively reflect deficiencies across hardware, procedural, and human factor dimensions. Hardware gaps include the limited adoption of mechanical rope cutters and shaft protection devices that could mitigate or prevent full propulsion loss upon initial fouling contact. Procedural shortfalls are evident in the absence of robust pre-passage planning protocols that incorporate real-time or shared spatial data on static gear locations, and in the lack of standardised night watchkeeping requirements commensurate with the elevated hazard density of inshore fishing grounds. Environmental conditions, particularly moderate to rough sea states, compound these deficiencies by increasing net drift unpredictability and reducing the detectability of surface markers through wave obscuration. A sailing vessel lost propulsion due to propeller wrap losing engine power, vessel anchored using emergency anchor. No injuries and no further damage to vessel.

representative_cases.description: Fishing vessel had issues with its propeller so the crew decided to stop the engine in order to prevent damage. They requested a tow and no further damages have been reported.

title: Propulsion Failure in Adverse Conditions, Single-Engine Vessels
description:
This theme is characterised by the catastrophic convergence of mechanical propulsion failure and hazardous operating conditions aboard recreational and small commercial vessels. The majority of incidents (53 machinery-related) involve single-engine vessels that lose all motive power, leaving them beam-on to wind and sea, adrift in shipping lanes, or subject to progressive flooding risk. These incidents recur because operators routinely accept the latent vulnerability of single-engine configurations without adequate compensatory measures — no redundant propulsion, no functioning sailing backup, and no credible contingency plan. Compounding factors such as night-time departure, solo operation, and unseaworthy vessel condition transform what might be a manageable mechanical deficiency into a life-threatening emergency requiring external search and rescue intervention. The decision to operate in deteriorating weather or at night without verified propulsion reliability is a consistent antecedent across this cluster.

The latent hardware failures at the root of these incidents — undetected fuel contamination, deferred maintenance, worn or neglected engine components, and overall vessel disrepair — reflect a systemic failure of pre-departure safety culture rather than isolated mechanical misfortune. Human factors including overconfidence, normalisation of deferred maintenance, and inadequate voyage planning are pervasive, with operators frequently departing without verifying that primary or backup systems are serviceable. Environmental conditions, particularly night-time sea states of Sea State 3–4, moderate winds, cold temperatures, and swell, do not cause these incidents but act as force multipliers that compress the time available for self-rescue and dramatically increase the severity of crew harm, as evidenced by cases of hypothermia and beam-on rolling risk. The consistent absence of adequate anchoring capability, working sailing rigs, or secondary propulsion means that once the engine fails, the vessel and crew are entirely dependent on external SAR resources, creating preventable risk to both survivors and rescuers.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (machinery maintenance and propulsion reliability requirements)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (distress signalling, lifejackets, and survival equipment for loss-of-propulsion emergencies)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (voyage planning, weather assessment, and safe manning obligations including contingency preparedness)
preventable_by: Mandate documented pre-departure engine checks and periodic maintenance records for all registered recreational vessels operating beyond sheltered waters, with enforceable minimum standards for fuel system integrity, cooling systems, and drive components, enforced through targeted compliance audits at departure points.

Require single-engine vessels operating at night or in offshore or exposed waters to carry a functional secondary means of propulsion (such as an auxiliary outboard, a serviceable sailing rig, or a verified anchor system appropriate to local depths), and include verification of these systems as part of any mandatory safety briefing or vessel registration renewal process.

Implement mandatory passage planning and weather check requirements for night-time recreational departures, including a formal go/no-go decision framework that accounts for forecast sea state, wind strength, crew numbers, vessel propulsion configuration, and available SAR coverage, supported by accessible public guidance and enforced through marina operator and harbour authority briefing obligations.

A recreational motor vessel experienced engine failure whilst fishing. The single crew member on board called for help and a lifeboat was dispatched to tow them back to port. There were no injuries, pollution or damage.

A sailing vessel with an auxiliary engine experienced propulsion failure. The crew could not raise the sail due to the fact that the vessel was in poor condition. Another vessel evacuated the two crew on board and brought them ashore. The crew of that vessel attempted to set up a tow but due to the condition of the casualty vessel, it was not successful. The vessel was abandoned and left to drift.

Recreational motor-cruiser experienced engine failure and was drifting in a shipping lane. The vessel crew issued a distress signal. Once assistance arrived they realised one of the crewmembers was in need of medical attention as they had cold hands, warm face and were feeling seasick. The crewmember was fully alert the entire time and shivering. They brought the crewmember ashore for further trea

A recreational motor vessel experienced engine failure possibly through debris in the fuel. The vessel was unable to anchor as it required the engine to bring the anchor in but the vessel was in deep water and able to drift. A search and rescue (SAR) was tasked to the stricken vessel. One SAR crew member went onboard to assist the lone skipper and set up a tow, the vessel was then towed to a harbo

A fishing vessel had an engine failure, another fishing vessel started to tow the casualty vessel but was struggling to make any headway. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to tow the fishing vessel back to shore.

A fishing vessel with two crew onboard experienced an engine failure, the vessel was drifting towards the a breakwater entrance until the crew anchored. As there were no vessels in the area to assist and location of the fishing vessel, a search and rescue (SAR) vessel towed the fishing vessel to shore. No reported crew injuries or visual vessel damage.

A commercial sight seeing motor boat experienced propulsion failure whilst out on an excursion. They dropped anchor and called for help using the VHF. Two search and rescue vessels launched. One extracted the passengers and brought them ashore whilst the other one set up a tow and brought the vessel back to harbour. There were no injuries, pollutions or damages.

A recreational fishing vessel experienced an engine failure at a harbour entrance. The two crew onboard attempted to restart the engine but were unsuccessful. The vessel was anchored and the crew waited for a search and rescue (SAR) vessel to tow the stricken vessel into harbour. No reported injuries or vessel damage.

A sailing yacht was on passage when it had an engine failure and later becalmed due to the light winds. A PANPAN was broadcasted however due to heavy traffic in the area, a search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to tow the yacht to shore. No reported damage.

title: Electrical Hardware Failures Escalating to Fire Through Maintenance Gaps
description:
This theme is characterised by small but operationally significant fire events initiated by latent electrical hardware defects — including overheating transformers, degraded plugs, faulty pre-heaters, defective valve spindles, and failed alternators — across a diverse range of vessel types and operational contexts. In every case, the immediate ignition event was preceded by an undetected component-level fault that had been allowed to develop within a preventive maintenance regime insufficiently rigorous to identify degraded or substandard equipment before failure. The high proportion of procedural and hardware contributing factors confirms that these incidents are not random or unforeseeable; they represent a systemic pattern in which maintenance programmes are not effectively closing the gap between scheduled sign-off and the actual physical condition of electrical and mechanical components returned to service. The recurrence of this pattern across vessel classes suggests an industry-wide normalisation of risk around electrical maintenance standards.

Latent hardware vulnerabilities — components operating beyond their thermal rating, with degraded insulation, or in environmentally challenging enclosures — form the foundational failure mode in this cluster. These defects are inherently difficult to detect without component-level inspection protocols that go beyond visual checks or operational testing alone. Human factors, including reduced crew vigilance during night-time operations and the implicit trust placed in maintenance records over physical verification, further reduce the likelihood of pre-failure identification. Environmental conditions such as condensation in cold cabinets or elevated ambient temperatures in machinery spaces accelerate degradation while simultaneously reducing inspection frequency. Critically, the absence of overtemperature protection devices across multiple incidents indicates a systemic gap in engineering safeguards: where prevention fails, no secondary barrier exists to arrest escalation before smoke, alarm activation, or open fire. Effective mitigation requires both improved component-level inspection standards and the mandatory fitting of thermal protection on susceptible electrical circuits.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Structure, Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (Part D – Electrical Installations)
SOLAS Chapter II-2: Construction – Fire Protection, Fire Detection and Fire Extinction
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (as applicable to operational readiness and watchkeeping standards supporting hazard detection)
preventable_by: Implement component-level electrical inspection protocols within Planned Maintenance Systems (PMS) that require physical verification of insulation integrity, thermal ratings, and component condition — not solely operational testing or visual sign-off — for all electrical heating elements, transformers, alternators, and plug/socket assemblies at defined intervals appropriate to their operating environment.

Mandate the installation and periodic testing of overtemperature protection devices (thermal fuses, thermal cutouts, or temperature-monitored circuit breakers) on electrical circuits associated with heating equipment, transformers, and high-load components, ensuring that a secondary engineered safeguard arrests fault escalation before ignition can occur.

Require flag state and classification society audits to include targeted review of electrical maintenance records against the physical condition of high-risk components, with particular scrutiny of equipment operating in thermally or environmentally challenging enclosures (e.g., cold rooms, machinery spaces), to close the gap between documented maintenance compliance and actual equipment serviceability.

title: Propeller Fouling from Inadequate Situational Awareness and Planning
description:
This theme encompasses incidents where vessels suffered propeller fouling or entanglement due to a convergence of inadequate pre-operational planning, insufficient situational awareness, and degraded environmental conditions. The incidents span charter yachts fouling mooring infrastructure, fishing vessels entangling their own or adjacent gear, and vessels encountering derelict seabed debris — yet all share a common latent architecture: the hazard was foreseeable, the conditions that activated it were predictable, and the procedural or physical safeguards that could have interrupted the causal chain were absent or ineffective. Night-time operations appear as a consistent aggravating factor, systematically reducing the crew's ability to visually detect submerged lines, gear proximity, or debris before contact. Tidal constraints, wind, and sea state further eroded the margins available for corrective action, converting manageable risks into propulsion emergencies requiring SAR intervention.

The hardware dimension of this theme is characterised by the near-universal absence of mechanical mitigations such as rope cutters or propeller protection devices, meaning that once contact with a line or net occurred, escalation to full fouling was essentially inevitable. Human factors are prominent in the form of single-handed operation, inadequate lookout arrangements, and failures to complete pre-departure or pre-approach checks — including securing trawl gear, surveying mooring field layouts, and accounting for tidal effects on navigable clearance. At the procedural level, there is a consistent absence of formal protocols governing navigation through mooring fields at night, departure clearance confirmation from adjacent vessels, and gear management relative to the propeller during fishing operations. Together, these latent conditions — hardware deficiency, human capacity limitations, and procedural gaps — repeatedly align under adverse environmental circumstances to produce outcomes that are both foreseeable and preventable.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulations 19, 31, and 34: navigational watchkeeping, distress situations, and voyage/passage planning)
SOLAS Chapter XI-1 – Special Measures to Enhance Maritime Safety (Regulation 4: port State control and inspection of operational readiness)
SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (Regulation 19: emergency training and drills, including response to propulsion loss and SAR coordination)
preventable_by: Mandate formal pre-approach and pre-departure checklists for operations in or adjacent to mooring fields, requiring documented confirmation of mooring infrastructure layout, tidal clearance assessment, and securing of all deployed gear before manoeuvring — with particular procedural rigour enforced for night-time operations.

Require the fitting of rope cutters or shaft protection devices on all commercial fishing vessels and charter vessels as a baseline engineering control, reducing the probability that incidental line contact escalates to full propeller fouling and loss of propulsion.

Establish minimum safe manning and watchkeeping standards for night-time fishing and berthing operations that prohibit single-handed vessel management during high-risk evolutions such as gear retrieval, mooring field transit, or confined berth departure, ensuring a dedicated lookout is maintained whenever the vessel is manoeuvring in proximity to potential fouling hazards.

representative_cases.description: A charter sailing yacht with four crew onboard was preparing to moor when the yacht passed over another mooring and fouled the propeller. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to assist, the SAR crew cut the mooring as they were unable to free the line from the propeller and towed the yacht to another mooring. A diver was organised for the following to remove the line. No crew injuries or re

A fishing vessel was departing its berth when its trawl door became entangled in the mooring ropes of another fishing vessel still alongside. The vessel collided with the moored fishing vessel and when attempting to break free caused the moored vessel to strike the quay wall. The crew of the fishing vessel that was underway, boarded the moored fishing vessel and freed the trawl door from the moori

A stern trawler fouled its propeller on its gear and was unable to free the propeller without help. Search and rescue assets were launched to help the single occupant of the vessel by towing it back to port where the propeller was freed.

A fishing vessel was recovering static fishing gear from the seabed when the propeller became fouled by an old net, rope or similar debris. The fishing vessel broadcast an alert and the local lifeboat launched and assisted the vessel back to port with a tow. All people on board were fit and well with lifejackets and there were no injuries, pollution or damages to the vessel.

A sailing vessel experienced propulsion failure after it fouled its propeller in the shipping lane at the entrance to a port. They transmitted an urgency call for assistance and another nearby vessel rendered assistance, setting up a tow and taking them into the harbour to a safe mooring. The vessel could not sail or use their engine.

A hospital/ambulance vessel (patient transport) grounded after the jet units became clogged by seaweed causing propulsion failure. The skipper reported that the seaweed got into the jets due to not keeping an effective enough look out to sight the seaweed and take evasive action.

A fishing vessel experienced propulsion failure after the propeller became fouled in the rope of its fishing gear. The vessel could not be freed by towage alone and a search and rescue vessel was dispatched with the appropriate equipment to free the fouled propeller. The search and rescue vessel towed the fishing vessel back to port. There were no injuries, damages or pollution.

A search and rescue hovercraft was returning from a tasking when it contacted with a lobster pot which resulted in the port side of the skirting. This caused significant damage that meant that the vessel had to go out of service to complete repairs. The commander of the vessel also injured their thumb requiring first aid treatment.

A passenger vessel experienced propulsion failure after a lobster pot line fouled one of the propellers. It pulled out the propeller shaft from the starboard propulsion meaning that the vessel was only able to manoeuvre on one engine. The crew decided to request a tow back to port from another vessel as a safety precaution.

A single hand fishing catamaran became entangled in ropes from lobster and crab pots close to the shore and fouled the propeller. The vessel dropped an anchor to try to prevent it moving onto the rocks and called for help. The local lifeboat service attended and set up a tow to bring it back to port.

title: Steering System Failures Due to Latent Mechanical Deficiencies
description:
This theme is characterised by the failure of primary steering systems — spanning hydraulic components, control circuits, and mechanical linkages — across a range of vessel types and operational contexts, with failures consistently occurring during high-demand phases of navigation such as berthing, channel transit, and manoeuvring under pilotage. The recurrence of these incidents points to a systemic pattern in which latent hardware degradation, including seal deterioration, hose failure, and hydraulic system faults, develops over time without detection, only manifesting catastrophically at the moment of greatest navigational risk. While emergency steering systems and contingency procedures are generally available and, in most cases, successfully employed, their activation represents a reactive response to a failure that should have been identified and rectified through proactive maintenance and rigorous pre-arrival functional testing. The persistence of this pattern across vessel classes and operators indicates that maintenance regimes and inspection protocols for steering systems are frequently inadequate, deferred, or insufficiently documented.

Latent hardware deficiencies are the dominant causative factor in this theme, with steering component degradation going undetected due to gaps in preventive maintenance programmes and insufficient adherence to SOLAS-mandated steering gear test requirements prior to arrival in port. Human factors compound the risk: bridge teams and operators demonstrate variable competency in executing rapid switchover to emergency steering modes, and pre-arrival checks are inconsistently applied, reducing the opportunity to detect faults before they become critical. Environmental conditions — particularly night-time operations, moderate winds, and confined waterways — consistently amplify the consequences of steering failures by compressing crew reaction time, reducing situational awareness, and limiting sea room for vessel recovery. The convergence of undetected hardware deterioration, procedural non-compliance, and adverse operating conditions creates the conditions for escalation from a manageable equipment fault to a serious marine incident involving channel exceedance, collision risk, or grounding.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1 (Part E) — Steering Gear: Construction, Testing, and Operational Requirements including pre-departure and pre-arrival steering gear tests
SOLAS Chapter V — Safety of Navigation: Bridge procedures, navigational watchkeeping, and pilot-bridge information exchange during critical manoeuvres
SOLAS Chapter IX — Management for the Safe Operation of Ships: ISM Code obligations for planned maintenance systems, defect reporting, and emergency preparedness
preventable_by: Mandate and verify full functional testing of all steering modes — including emergency and non-follow-up systems — at every pre-arrival check in compliance with SOLAS Regulation II-1/29, with completed test records signed by the Officer of the Watch and retained for inspection by port State control and flag State auditors.

Implement a structured, condition-based planned maintenance programme for all hydraulic steering system components — including hoses, seals, actuators, and fittings — with defined inspection intervals, replacement life limits, and mandatory defect escalation procedures under the ISM Code to prevent latent degradation from reaching operational failure.

Conduct regular, scenario-based emergency steering drills that require bridge teams and pilots to practise rapid switchover to emergency and non-follow-up steering control under simulated time pressure, with performance outcomes recorded and used to inform refresher training, ensuring effective crew response when primary steering failure occurs during critical phases of navigation.

representative_cases.description: A survey vessel had a hydraulic oil leak from the steering system. The leakage was contained on board in the machinery space. The vessel was able to make it to port using their emergency steering system.

Cargo vessel was approaching a dock when it was noted that a steering control failure had occurred. Navigation control was shifted back to the center conning position for the final approach to the berth. The pilot tug leaned back and increased from half to full - used to arrest the forward motion. The gain for the steering control did not occur until it was switched into emergency push button oper

A bulk carrier under pilotage experienced steering failure and left the channel, possibly grounding. The vessel was able to be freed from the area with the assistance of tugs that were already made fast. It left the port and went to anchorage for investigations.

It was reported that a hire cruiser had a steering failure, a passing cruiser encountered the hire cruiser broadside across a river. The vessel had a steering failure to both the upper and lower helm positions. Using the bow thruster and the engine the crew managed to reach the bank, disembark and secure the vessel to the bank. No reported injuries to crew.

A replenishment tanker was manoeuvring with a pilot on board when a technical malfunction caused steering failure. The crew switched over to the non follow up steering control. There was no damage or pollution.

A cargo vessel was preparing to depart a berth, the planned manoeuvre was to conduct a swing and proceed downriver. There was a strong tidal stream with the flooding tide. The navigation was passed to the pilot, after releasing the spring line, the engine was stopped, the rudder was hard to starboard position and the current continued to turn the vessel. The pilot gave the command full ahead, soon

A domestic ro-pax vessel was departing from port when one of the rudders became stuck hard to one side due to mechanical issues. The vessel was able to maintain its heading until the tugs arrive to assist the vessel back to port so that the malfunction could be rectified. There were no damages, pollution or injuries.

A foot passenger vessel was departing from the berth when it experience failure of the primary steering control. The crew switched over to the secondary steering, which also didn't work so they proceeded to switch over to the third steering option of the emergency tiller. They managed to manoeuvre back to the berth but on attempting to berth stern first control was lost and the tide pulled the ves

A cruise ship was departing port with two tugs in attendance. It was reported that the steering was non-responsive and a slow rate of turn to port, corrective action was made using the thrusters. The cruise ship developed a slow starboard swing, the speed was reduced and the forward tug was asked to push to maintain a safe position in the channel. The emergency steering procedures were initiated,

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was returning to station, as the vessel was proceeding to the carriage there was minimal output from the steering inputs. The helm applied power resulting in the engines going hard over, the vessel veered away and the stern came into contact with the side of the carriage. The propellers struck the rubber carriage bed, causing damage to the propellors. The vessel wa

representative_cases.description: Pleasure craft was engaged in fishing when a tug proceeded towards the vessel and collided with it. No injuries and/or damages reported from both vessels,
A sailing yacht had a near miss with a tanker. The sailing yacht impeded the tankers passage, the ships whistle was used but no response from the yacht forcing the tanker to take avoiding action.
A sailing yacht had a close quarters situation with a tanker. The pilot on board the tanker reported that the yacht cut across the bow of the tanker. The approximate distance was two cables.
An angling charter vessel was at anchor when a yacht came down the starboard side and across the anchor rope and missed the beam by ten feet. There was no damage or injuries.
A containership on passage and a sailing yacht had a close quarters situation whilst the containership was constrained to a deep water route.
One sailing vessel was on a mooring when a second sailing vessel collided with them and left the scene. The vessel on a mooring reported only superficial damage. No injuries reported.
A fishing vessel had a near miss COLREG infringement as the skipper fell asleep at the helm.
Chemical tanker was impeded by a fishing vessel who passed approximately 6 cables ahead. VTS contacted the fishing vessel to keep well clear. No damages/injuries sustained.
Two fishing vessels collided with each other minor damage, no injuries. No reports of injuries or water ingress. Vessels made way to port.
A sailing yacht had a near miss, close quarters, with a tanker. The sailing yacht impeded the tankers passage as it was outbound from a port.

representative_cases.description: A general cargo vessel was transiting through a lock when it made contact with the edge of the lock. This resulted in a dent and scraping on the hull of the vessel and a crack to the coping stones.
A ro-pax vessel made contact with an outer lock gate on arrival into port. No reported damage.
A general cargo vessel made contact with a lock side wall whilst manoeuvring. The vessel was coming into the lock on the flood.
Service operational vessel whilst it had pass through the lock at a dock ,it contacted east outer floodgate causing some damage to wooden fender in the lock and forwards rubber fender on the vessel.
Tug was running up in the lock when it lifted a coping stone , when it had dropped back into place, but it was still protruding slightly and would need resealing.
A hopper dredger made contact with a lock gate and sustained bends to safety rails and ladders on its port side.
Whilst making fast bow to bow with the cargo vessel, the tug made contact with the ships port bow. This caused the tug to lose position and manoeuvre away from the ship resulting in mast damage.
A general cargo vessel under pilotage made contact with a lock wall during a lock entry manoeuvre. The vessel sustained superficial damage the lock wall and a fender were also damaged.
A general cargo vessel was entering a lock when the vessel made contact with the inner burleigh wheel, resulting in in vessel starboard side rails bending.

A general cargo vessel was entering a lock when it landed on the roller fender in the lock. The vessel sustained damage to the handrails on the starboard side, no injuries to crew and no damage reported to the dock infrastructure.

Port tender had an engine issue and was not available for berthing. No further defects reported.

Pilot boat suffered an electrical failure. The vessel still had propulsion but limited steerage. No further defects were reported.
Ro-Ro cargo's portside engine failed, however they were able to make their way back to port without any assistance.

The guest remained on board for the remain

An inspection of the area was carried out with no defects or haza

Cruise ship's passenger was approaching the door when they tripped on the door ramp and fell landing face down on their left shoulder. The casualty sustained a shoulder dislocation, soft tissue injury to upper lip and a chip to a tooth. No defects found in the area.

A cruise ship was alongside in port when a passenger tripped on the threshold of a fire screen door and fell to the deck. The passenger sustained a fractured radius in their right arm. They were wearing flip flops with a thick sole at the time of the accident.

A cruise ship passenger tripped on a tripod stand that was obscured by a curtain. They fell to the floor. Medical staff attended and took them to the medical centre for evaluation and treatment. The passenger sustained a sprain.

  "description": "This theme is characterised by man overboard (MOB) events occurring predominantly during night-time sailing passages and racing, where darkness, moderate sea states, and deck instability combine to create disproportionately fatal outcomes. The incidents are unified not by severe environmental conditions — several occurred in relatively benign weather — but by a persistent failure to enforce personal safety equipment compliance, particularly harness and tether systems, among all crew members. The racing context emerges as a critical systemic driver: competitive culture normalises non-compliance with safety protocols, and the contrast between tethered survivors and untethered fatalities in the same events illustrates how preventable these outcomes are. The pattern recurs because the latent causes are cultural and organisational rather than technical, meaning equipment availability alone does not translate into equipment use. Recovery outcomes are heavily dependent on luck — proximity of other vessels, rapid response — rather than reliable, trained, and rehearsed MOB procedures.\n\nLatent human factors dominate this theme, including complacency during calm conditions, inadequate pre-voyage safety briefings, and the normalisation of risk within competitive sailing culture. representative_cases.description: Sailing yacht crewmember presumed deceased after falling overboard during a race.
MOB from yacht. Unknown vessel recovered the casualty and returned it to the yacht. No injuries sustained.
The vessel was found to be drifting with no persons onboard with the engine running and signs of recent occupancy. Crew member that was missing from vessel when located and was confirmed as deceased.
MOB from a yacht, MOB recovered onto a RIB and is safe and well. No further information provided.

A sailing yacht was taking part in a race when the two crew were thrown overboard. The skipper was tethered and was able to recover to the yacht. The other crewmember was recovered by a nearby fishing vessel and taken by helicopter to hospital where he was declared deceased. The skipper and yacht were towed to port by rescue services.
Man overboard from a yacht. The casualty was pulled aboard and no injuries were reported.
Initially reported as a man-overboard by NFCA on the basis of interrupted phone call. No evidence to support person went in the water.
Sailing vessel crewmember fell overboard during a race. They were recovered by the vessel, which continued on its passage.  Time in water: 3 minutes
A sailing vessel crew member went overboard resulting in missing presumed fatal, another crew member injured.

A recreational sailing vessel had a person over board, recovered with no injuries, no further assistance needed. Both persons were safe and well on board after recovering one from the water and the AIS MOB was switched off. WE HAD A MOB BUT THEYRE BACK ON BOARD.

title: Undetected Hull and Machinery Deficiencies Causing Flooding
description:
This theme is characterised by flooding incidents arising from latent structural and mechanical deficiencies — including failed through-hull fittings, degraded hoses, deteriorated pump casings, and compromised hull integrity — that were not identified prior to the flooding event. The incidents span recreational vessels, commercial fishing vessels, and SAR platforms, indicating the issue is not confined to any single sector. A consistent pattern emerges whereby the underlying physical vulnerability had been developing over time through corrosion, material fatigue, rot, or wear, yet no pre-departure or routine inspection regime was in place or sufficiently rigorous to detect it. Once flooding commenced, inadequate onboard bilge pumping capacity, the absence of damage control materials, and — in several cases — the vessel being operated at night or the crew being asleep, allowed water ingress to progress to a critical or life-threatening stage before effective intervention was possible.

Latent hardware deficiencies represent the foundational cause across this cluster, but their consequences were consistently amplified by compounding human and environmental factors. Absent or inadequate maintenance and pre-departure seaworthiness checks reflect systemic failures in safety culture and regulatory compliance, particularly among recreational vessel operators. Human factors — including limited crew knowledge of damage control procedures, delayed detection due to night-time operations, and sole operators being asleep — removed opportunities for early intervention at each stage of the flooding sequence. Environmental conditions, including rough seas and confused swell, introduced additional mechanical stress and complicated emergency response. The recurrence of these incidents across vessel types and operational contexts demonstrates that without structured inspection regimes, minimum onboard damage control capability, and crew competency in emergency flooding response, latent hardware vulnerabilities will continue to manifest as serious or potentially fatal flooding events.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, and Machinery (watertight integrity, bilge pumping arrangements, and machinery maintenance requirements)
SOLAS Chapter II-2: Fire Protection, Detection and Extinction (by analogy, applied to damage control readiness and onboard emergency equipment standards)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (safe manning, operational readiness, and the master's responsibility for vessel seaworthiness before departure)
preventable_by: Mandate structured pre-departure seaworthiness checklists for all vessel classes — including inspection of through-hull fittings, seacocks, hoses, bilge alarms, and pump operational status — with records retained onboard and subject to periodic audit by flag state or port state control authorities.

Require all vessels to carry a minimum standard damage control kit (including soft patches, expanding plugs, hydraulic cement, and seacock closure tools) and ensure crew are trained and exercised in its use, with competency in identifying and isolating flooding sources forming part of mandatory safety familiarisation.

Implement enforceable planned maintenance system (PMS) requirements — scaled to vessel type and age — that include periodic inspection and replacement schedules for high-risk hull penetrations, pump casings, and associated fittings, with particular attention to wooden-hulled vessels and ageing mechanical components susceptible to corrosion and fatigue.

representative_cases.description: A recreational motor vessel began to flood and the two crewmembers on board called for assistance. A search and rescue vessel located the flooding vessel and disembarked the two crewmembers. The crew of the search and rescue vessel attempted to pump out the water but it was still coming in and they could not keep up with the ingress. They decided that they could do no more and left the vessel to s

Fishing vessel experienced flooding in the engine room as the result of the casing on a service pump breaking. The service pump in questioned pumped salt water to the deck wash pipe and could also be used to generate suction on the bilge system.

A wooden recreational motor vessel sprung a leak whilst it was moored alongside and began to progressively flood before foundering. The single crewmember who was inside the cabin sleeping at the time, was initially trapped but managed to make their way out to the wheelhouse roof and was rescued. They suffered the effects of hypothermia due to the night time air and the cold water but they were not

A sailing yacht had started to take on water, the lone skipper had noticed the ingress when they got up in the morning. A vessel in vicinity attended but was unable to assist, a search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to the yacht with their salvage pump. The ingress was under control and the yacht towed to a mooring as the skipper was fatigued.

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was being stood down from service when the bilge alarm sounded. There was water ingress in the engine room. Emergency salvage pumps were used to support the bilge pump in clearing the water ingress while the vessel made it's way back to the station. No injuries to any persons reported.

A fishing vessel started flooding following a crew mistakenly opening a seacock in port. Member of the crew accidentally opened a seacock which instigated the flooding incident. They had no engineering officer aboard.

The crew of a service vessel were carrying out ballasting when one of the crewmembers noted that the tanks were not overflowing when expected. The crew then found water ingress into the engine room. This resulted in partial power failure and the emergency generator automatically started. The main engine continued to operate. The crew then found that the water was coming from a manhole cover that d

A converted trawler was on passage when it began to take on water and the vessel's pumps could not keep up with the ingress. The two crew on board issued a mayday call and abandoned to their tender before being rescued by the local lifeboat service. The vessel partially sank, drifted and later broke up ashore. There were no injuries. There was pollution in the form of debris from the vessel.

A recreational motor vessel with four crew onboard starting taking on water. The vessel still had a working engine and headed to a slipway, the crew started bailing the water out. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel attended to assist with pumping out the water and then escorted the motor vessel to shore.

Lifeboat were tasked to a RHIB taking on water with 7 POB. One lifeboat recovered all persons on board, while another lifeboat towed the RHIB back. Ambulances assessed all casualties, who were confirmed to be in good health.

A consistent pattern emerges of crew working in close, unguarded proximity to high-energy mechanical hazards — winches, capstans, haulers, net drums, anchor chains, and mooring ropes under tension — often under time pressure and without adequate PPE. The recurrence of this incident type across vessel categories and operational contexts indicates entrenched systemic deficiencies in safety culture, machinery guarding standards, and task-level risk management rather than isolated failures. The frequency of helicopter evacuation and medevac responses further underscores that these injuries consistently occur in remote or offshore environments, where the consequences of inadequate preventive controls are compounded by limited access to timely medical care.\n\nLatent hardware deficiencies — principally the absence of or inadequate physical guarding on rotating and powered deck machinery — represent the most persistent engineering failure across this cluster. Where guarding exists, its removal or bypass during active operations is a recurrent human factors issue, often rationalised under operational efficiency pressures. Night-time operations are a near-universal contributing condition, reducing situational awareness, depth perception, and the crew member's ability to perceive nip points and entanglement hazards in real time. Adverse environmental conditions including elevated sea states, high winds, and vessel motion further degrade balance, manual dexterity, and judgement during high-risk manual tasks. Procedural failures — including the absence of task-specific risk assessments, non-enforcement of jewellery prohibition policies, PPE non-compliance, and insufficient crew training on machinery hazards — represent the human and organisational layer through which these latent risks are realised as injuries. A fishing vessels crew member hit head on a net drum resulting in a superficial head wound.

A fishing vessels crew member was hit with a crabbing pot resulting in a injury to the ribs and medivac'd from the vessel.
Fishing vessels crew member got struck by a lobster pot resulting in a cut to the forehead and a possible concussion.

title: Manual Tool and Catch Handling Injuries from Procedural Failures
description:
This theme is characterised by preventable lacerations, puncture wounds, and soft tissue injuries sustained during routine manual tasks involving knives and hazardous catch species aboard both fishing vessels and commercial ships. The incidents share a common thread of procedural non-compliance or absence, whether through unsafe cutting techniques directed toward the body, failure to wear mandated PPE such as cut-resistant gloves, or the handling of venomous species without species-specific safe handling protocols. The recurrence of these injuries strongly suggests systemic latent failures in safety culture, procedural enforcement, and hazard identification rather than isolated lapses in individual judgement. Normalised deviance — where the routine disregard of protective measures becomes culturally embedded and tacitly accepted — appears to be a primary driver across multiple operational contexts, from the fishing deck to the ship's galley.

Environmental conditions consistently compound these procedural shortcomings. Night-time operations conducted in significant wave heights and elevated wind speeds introduce vessel motion and reduced situational awareness, transforming otherwise manageable manual tasks into high-risk activities. The discrepancy identified between reported sea states and measured wave heights in several incidents raises additional concerns regarding accurate environmental risk assessment prior to task commencement. Human factors including fatigue, habitual bypass of safety requirements, and inadequate supervisory oversight further erode the already fragile procedural barriers. Hardware considerations, notably the absence of cut-resistant PPE, inadequate task lighting, and the lack of species-appropriate handling equipment, remove the last line of defence against injury. Collectively, these factors indicate that the barriers protecting crew from harm during manual handling tasks are insufficiently layered, poorly enforced, and rarely verified.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (crew safety procedures, emergency response, and onboard medical care obligations)
SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (safe working conditions, environmental risk assessment, and situational awareness obligations)
SOLAS Chapter XI-2 / ISM Code (Chapter IX) – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (safety management systems, procedural compliance, hazard identification, and normalised deviance prevention)
preventable_by: Implement and enforce mandatory task-specific safe working procedures for all knife use and hazardous catch handling operations, including a formal requirement to cut away from the body, use cut-resistant gloves and forearm protection, and apply species-specific handling protocols for venomous catch such as dogfish; compliance must be verified through regular drills and documented supervisory checks.

Introduce environmental go/no-go decision criteria for high-risk manual tasks conducted on deck or in the galley, requiring skippers and duty officers to formally assess wind speed, wave height, vessel motion, and lighting conditions before authorising cutting or hazardous handling operations at night or in adverse weather, with task postponement or additional control measures mandated when thresholds are exceeded.

Establish a structured PPE compliance assurance programme under the vessel's Safety Management System, including scheduled audits of glove and protective equipment availability and use in both deck and galley environments, mandatory reporting of PPE non-compliance as a near-miss event, and targeted safety culture interventions to address normalised deviance where protective equipment use has been routinely bypassed.

title: Crew Injury from Falls During Night Operations
description:
This theme is characterised by crew injuries resulting from falls, trips, and loss of balance occurring predominantly during night-time operations across a range of vessel types, from sailing yachts to offshore supply ships and replenishment tankers. The incidents share a consistent pattern: a crewmember is moving around or working on deck or below in reduced visibility conditions, often in adverse sea states, without adequate procedural controls governing safe movement or task execution. The consequences range from dislocated hips and broken limbs to head and neck trauma, and in several cases the injury itself generated secondary operational emergencies — most notably where a minimal crew complement was rendered unable to complete basic vessel handling after a single casualty. These incidents recur because the combination of night conditions, vessel motion, and structural or task-specific hazards is predictable yet insufficiently controlled at the procedural and organisational level.

The latent conditions underpinning this theme operate across all three principal factor domains. Environmentally, reduced night-time visibility degrades hazard perception, while moderate wind and sea states introduce vessel motion that directly destabilises crew footing and working posture. Hardware factors include fixed structural hazards such as canopy frames and open bilges positioned in proximity to crew movement and working areas, unguarded deck obstructions, and fittings that require excessive force to release — all of which amplify injury severity when a fall occurs. Human factors are consistently rooted in the absence of pre-task risk assessment, failure to adopt stable three-point contact postures or use available safety equipment such as jackstays and handholds, and inadequate situational awareness in low-light conditions. At the organisational level, the defining latent failure is the absence of robust procedural controls — including safe movement protocols for night passages, minimum safe manning risk assessments, and task-level hazard identification for routine maintenance — that would interrupt the chain of causation before injury occurs.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (safe movement, personal protective equipment, and crew safety procedures on deck)
SOLAS Chapter II-1 – Construction: Structure, Subdivision and Stability (structural arrangements, machinery spaces, and safeguarding of open bilges and fall hazards)
SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (passage planning, watchkeeping arrangements, and minimum safe manning considerations for short-handed operations)
preventable_by: Implement mandatory pre-departure and pre-task risk assessments for night passages and engine room maintenance that explicitly identify fall hazards, minimum safe crew manning requirements, deck obstructions, and the proximity of unguarded fall zones such as open bilges, with mitigations documented and briefed to all crew prior to commencement.

Establish and enforce a formal safe movement protocol for adverse conditions and night operations, requiring crew to use jackstays, tethers, and designated handholds when moving on deck, and mandating stable three-point contact postures and braced working positions when applying force to fittings or working near open machinery spaces.

Conduct routine vessel safety audits to identify and remediate fixed structural hazards in crew movement areas — including canopy frames, unguarded bilge openings, and deck obstructions — through physical guarding, high-visibility marking, improved task lighting in engine rooms and on deck, and updated vessel-specific risk registers that reflect the operational profile of short-handed or night passages.
