title: Crew Injuries During Stairway Transit in Adverse Conditions
description:
This theme is characterised by crew injuries sustained during routine stairway descent, where commonplace shipboard movement becomes a significant injury risk under the compounding influence of vessel motion, reduced night-time visibility, and behavioural lapses. Across incidents, the immediate causes vary — missed steps, slips, caught fingers, and loss of balance — but a consistent underlying pattern emerges: crew members are transiting stairways without adequate precaution, frequently in the absence of enforced safe movement protocols such as three-point contact or controlled descent speed. The recurrence of these incidents across vessel types, from passenger ships and ro-pax ferries to cargo vessels and dredgers, indicates that stairway transit injury is not an isolated or vessel-specific problem but a systemic safety failure rooted in the normalisation of risk during routine crew movement.

Latent hardware deficiencies play a material role in several incidents, including inadequate anti-slip surfaces, insufficient or poorly positioned handrails, and exposed structural hazards such as proud bulkhead panel seams adjacent to crew transit routes. Environmental conditions — particularly significant wave heights, Beaufort Force 5–6 winds, and night-time lighting limitations — substantially elevate the baseline risk, yet in several cases the recorded sea state data was inconsistent with measured wave heights, suggesting a failure in environmental situational awareness that may have led crews and officers to underestimate prevailing conditions. Human factors, including distraction, excessive descent speed, improper grip posture, and a failure to brace against vessel motion, further compound these vulnerabilities. The convergence of inadequate hardware safeguards, unreliable environmental reporting, and an organisational culture that does not effectively enforce safe movement procedures creates conditions in which injury during a routine transit between decks becomes a foreseeable and preventable outcome.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Structure, Subdivision and Stability (safe design of internal passageways, stairways, and structural hazard mitigation)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (crew safety procedures and emergency response to personnel injury)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code – safety management systems, procedural compliance, and risk assessment for routine operations)
preventable_by: Implement and enforce a mandatory Safe Movement Policy requiring three-point contact on all stairways during vessel movement, with compliance embedded in the Safety Management System, reinforced through regular drills, toolbox talks, and documented supervision by officers — with particular emphasis on night-time operations and adverse weather conditions.

Conduct structured hardware audits of all crew transit stairways, including inspection and remediation of anti-slip surface condition, handrail adequacy and positioning, and identification of structural snag hazards such as exposed panel seams or fittings; ensure corrective actions are tracked to completion and findings are incorporated into pre-voyage safety checks.

Introduce rigorous environmental situational awareness procedures requiring bridge officers to cross-validate logged sea state conditions against measured wave height and wind speed data, with mandatory dissemination of accurate weather and vessel motion advisories to all crew before and during periods of adverse conditions, prompting crew to apply heightened caution during movement throughout the vessel.

representative_cases.description: Passenger ship's crew member suffered a knee injury while descending the stairs. This did not result in a fall however, they managed to twist their leg and sprain their knee.  As a consequence of the accident, the crew member had to be medically disembarked.

Hopper dredger's crew member was walking down the stairs from the bridge to the mess room when they slipped and fell down the stairs. This fall resulted in bruised ribs and in an ankle luxation. The casualty was evacuated with the ship's crane on a stretcher and placed onto a lifeboat. The individual was taken for further medical assistance.

A ro-pax crew member caught their finger whilst descending a staircase resulting in a fracture and subsequent sign off from the vessel. There were no witnesses to the incident but investigations carried out by the vessel's safety officer concluded that the manner of descent contributed to the injury. The crew member had their fingers splayed whilst the descended at speed and reportedly caught them

A cruise ship crew member missed a step whilst descending a staircase and fractured their ankle. The crewmember reported that they were distracted whilst walking down the staircase. The area was inspected and no defects were found.

A general cargo vessel crewmember was descending a staircase in the engine room when they fell down. They hit their head on the way down sustaining superficial injuries such as bruises and cuts to the head, shoulder and chest. They were taken ashore for a check up at the hospital.

A cruise ships crew member slipped from ladder resulting in closed fracture to the leg. Descending the safety ladder from the upper bunk in there cabin facing forward, without holding the railings , when stepping down to the second last step, foot slipped off the edge of the step, striking there left ankle, and causing them to slip and slid down on to the floor.

An offshore support vessel crewmember was walking down a staircase with items in both of their hands when they fell. They were unable to arrest their fall and fell fourteen steps to the bottom. title: Lifting and Deck Operations: Inadequate Controls and Communication
description: This theme is characterised by a recurring failure to implement and enforce formalised safe work systems during high-risk deck operations involving lifting equipment, mooring machinery, and cargo handling. The incidents share a common pattern: personnel are exposed to hazardous energy — whether from suspended loads, tensioned lines, or moving crane equipment — without the protection of structured authorisation protocols, exclusion zones, or verified two-way communication. Operations proceed, or are allowed to continue, on the basis of informal verbal exchanges or individual judgement rather than binding procedural controls. This systemic reliance on informal coordination as the primary safety barrier means that a single lapse in attention, a misunderstood signal, or an unexpected equipment failure is sufficient to place crew members directly in harm's way with no secondary defence.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (maintenance of mechanical and mooring equipment)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (safe operational procedures and crew safety during deck operations)
SOLAS Chapter VI: Carriage of Cargoes (safe handling and stowage procedures, including cargo gear operation and personnel safety during cargo operations)
preventable_by: Mandate formalised Permit-to-Work and Lock-Out/Tag-Out protocols for all lifting, crane, and mooring operations, requiring explicit written authorisation before work commences and a verified two-way all-clear confirmation before any suspended load is moved or machinery is restarted, with no operation permitted to resume on the basis of informal verbal communication alone.

Require documented pre-operation equipment inspections and formal lift plans for all non-routine lifting operations, including independent verification of load ratings, rigging integrity, and hook and attachment condition, with mandatory establishment and enforcement of physical exclusion zones that prohibit personnel from entering drop, recoil, or crush areas during any phase of the lift or line-handling operation.

Implement mandatory contingency stop-work procedures for critical equipment failures during active operations — such as winch power loss during mooring — that automatically halt the operation and prohibit defaulting to unsafe manual handling, supported by regular drills so that crew responses to mid-operation failures are trained, rehearsed, and not left to individual improvisation under pressure.

title: Pending MAIB Investigation Incidents Lacking Substantive Data
description:
This cluster is characterised not by a shared causal pattern among incidents, but by a common data deficiency: all 23 incidents are represented solely by placeholder references to pending MAIB investigation reports, with no substantive narrative, causal analysis, vessel activity details, or outcome information available. The incidents span a range of severity classifications from Less Serious to Very Serious, and occurred predominantly under night-time conditions across varying sea states, from calm (Sea State 0) to rough (Sea State 5, 2.5–4 m). Without access to the underlying investigation findings, it is not possible to determine whether these incidents share genuine latent causes or represent a diverse cross-section of marine occurrences. The recurrence of this data gap across 23 cases highlights a systemic issue in the completeness of incident records held prior to the publication of formal investigation reports.

In the absence of substantive incident details, no meaningful determination can be made regarding the role of latent hardware or software failures, human factors, or environmental conditions in contributing to these events. The consistent notation of night-time conditions across the cluster warrants cautious consideration of visibility and lighting as potential contextual factors, and the presence of several incidents occurring in rough sea conditions (Sea State 5) may indicate that environmental factors warrant examination once full reports are available. The primary concern this cluster raises is an analytical and governance one: the absence of interim safety information in pending investigation records limits the ability of safety managers, port authorities, and fleet operators to act on early signals. It is strongly recommended that classification and thematic analysis of all 23 incidents be revisited and revised upon publication of the respective MAIB investigation reports.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulation 31 & 32: Danger messages and casualty reporting obligations)
SOLAS Chapter XI-1 – Special Measures to Enhance Maritime Safety (Regulation 6: Casualty investigation)
SOLAS Chapter I – General Provisions (Regulation 21: Casualty reporting and investigation requirements)
preventable_by: Establish a mandatory interim safety data requirement whereby flag states and administrations must record minimum substantive incident details (vessel type, activity, preliminary cause category, and immediate outcome) in official databases at the time of initial notification, independent of and prior to the completion of a full MAIB or equivalent investigation report.

Implement a structured incident triage protocol within marine casualty reporting systems that prevents the submission of placeholder-only records for serious and very serious incidents, requiring at minimum a preliminary factual summary and an initial contributing factor hypothesis to be completed by the reporting officer within 72 hours of the occurrence.

Develop and mandate the use of a standardised interim investigation disclosure framework, under which investigating authorities such as the MAIB publish preliminary safety findings or safety bulletins within a defined period following a serious or very serious incident, ensuring that actionable safety intelligence is disseminated to the maritime community without awaiting the completion of the full investigation report.

Passenger ships crew member fell from bunk ladder resulting in closed fractures to the hand. Crew members foot slipped from bunk ladder and fell, impacting there hand that was holding a mobile phone.

```json
{
  "title": "Electrical Safety and Energy Isolation Failures in Machinery Operations",
  "description": "This theme is characterised by crew injuries arising from direct or proximate contact with energised electrical systems and rotating machinery, where the immediate causes — electric shock, arc flash, barotrauma, and mechanical entrapment — are consistently enabled by the absence or defeat of established safe-work controls. Across all representative incidents, the recurring thread is not equipment failure in isolation, but the systematic non-enforcement of energy isolation disciplines: crewmembers work on live panels without mandatory PPE, reset faulted breakers without first confirming fault clearance, override lockout/tagout controls without authorisation, and engage machinery without verifying safe pre-conditions. This pattern strongly indicates a normalised culture of risk acceptance around live and energised systems, where procedural shortcuts have become embedded in routine practice and are no longer perceived as deviations. The incidents recur because the safety management system fails to translate written procedures into enforced, verified behaviour at the point of work.\n\nLatent hardware factors — including the absence of physical interlocks, multi-lock LOTO devices, arc flash guarding, and startup warning systems — remove the last engineered barriers that might otherwise prevent human error from producing injury. representative_cases.description: Oil tanker's crew member was rigging the bosun chair and showing how to lower the manila rope when they fell on their posterior side of the pelvic region. The individual took 7 days off from work.
A fishing vessel crew member fell down a staircase on board and fractured their knee. The vessel returned to port where a shore side ambulance attended and took the injured crewmember to hospital.
A suction dredger crewmember suffered a fractured leg after slipping and falling onboard while alongside. Ambulance and CRT arrived on scene, stabilised the casualty and extracted them to shore.
Inland tanker's crew member lost their balance and fell from the bridge deck onto the vessels main deck (approximately 2.5 metres)
A work boat crew member struck their ankle on a steel frame whilst working. They were signed off work for 8 days with a sprain.
Fishing vessel crewmember fell down stairs resulting in a fractured wrist. The crew member was landed ashore and taken to hospital for treatment.
A crewmember had a fall on-board a yacht which resulted in a puncture wound to their arm.
Whilst mooring a motor cruiser, one of the crew members slipped and fell into the hold resulting in a minor head injury.
Inland waterways vessel crew member tripped, resulting in sprained ankle. Crew moored/berthed towed barge to moorings, one crewmember tripped when making way back to the vessel and twisted ankle.
An angling vessel crewmember was injured when the vessel hit a wave causing the crewmember to fall to the deck and dislocate their shoulder.

Overnight the anchor dragged resulting in the vessel grounding on a reef and requiring assistance. The vessel was lifted for an inspection and damage to the keel was found. The tide was rising, so the vessel's crew waited and deployed the anchor. The skipper was able to

A charter yacht with five persons on board lost engine power and grounded on a sandbank. The vessel sustained minor damage and no injuries were reported. A lifeboat and CRT were tasked but the casualty was taken under tow by another vessel. The casualty was moored to the pontoons with the assistance of lifeboat and CRT.

A general cargo vessel was on approach to a port a pilot embarked for the arrival. During pilotage the vessel experienced a black, resulting in reduced manoeuvrability due to a lack of power to the steering gear. The vessels pitch was reduced however the vessel was still moved forwards. An anchor was dropped to try and slow the vessel, but the vessel grounded. The main engine was stopped and the d

Approaching a planned anchorage.  Decided to anchor 80m short of planned position as it appeared there were more mooring buoys ahead than on previous visits.  Plan was to lower 30m of cable. Cable jammed in hawse pipe with 10m out and anchor on seabed.  Gentle astern motion did not cause anchor cable to pay out and while attempting to resolve the issue and recover the anchor to relay we had drifte

A search and rescue vessel was manoeuvring close to the shore to land a crew member on the rocks when it grounded. The skipper of the vessel was attempting to move away from the shore but the swell was hampering the movement of the vessel and the tilt function of the engines was not operating properly. The crew manually lowered the engines and the vessel was able to move away from the rocks, but n

A recreational motor vessel had anchored 100 – 200m off a beach and switched off the engine. The crew realised the conditions were not as calm as expected and decided to return to harbour. One crew member pulled up the anchor and attempted to re-start engine but it wouldn’t start, they did not try to anchor again. Phoned for assistance but the vessel had already grounded by the time a search and r

A fishing vessel fouled its propeller with fishing gear, attempted to anchor and the anchor was lost due to a cable snapping. The vessel grounded on a nearby rocky shore. Search and rescue assets attended and found the crew safe and well. The vessel was re-floated on the next high tide with the assistance of a lifeboat. The vessel was towed into port by the lifeboat.

A small sailing vessel with four crew drifted aground after having engine failure as there was not enough wind to sail. The crew were recovered ashore by a lifeboat. The casualty vessel was later towed into harbour by a private vessel when the vessel re-floated. There were no injuries or reported damage.

The vessel's anchor had started to drag, the master electric switch had burnt out and there was no power to the winch. The anchor was potentially not out enough and as a result the vessel grounded onto rocks. The lifeboat towed them off the rocks and assisted the vessel back into port. No damage or water ingress was reported.

title: Undetected Hull Ingress Pathways and Inadequate Bilge Safeguards
description:
This theme is characterised by flooding incidents arising from latent deficiencies in hull integrity systems — principally failed or deteriorating through-hull fittings, exhaust system components, and seacocks — that go undetected until water ingress has reached a critical level. A consistent pattern across incidents is the absence of any effective first line of defence: bilge pumps are undersized or non-functional, bilge alarms are absent or inoperable, and pre-departure or routine maintenance inspections have failed to identify the degradation of components that are known high-risk ingress pathways. Where vessels are left unattended at berth without watchkeeping arrangements or remote monitoring, the situation is further compounded, with chance observation by members of the public becoming the sole means of detection. The incidents span both underway and alongside contexts, indicating that the underlying procedural and equipment deficiencies are systemic rather than situational.

Latent hardware failure is the proximate cause in the majority of cases within this cluster, but the severity and frequency of these incidents is sustained by compounding human and environmental factors. Inadequate familiarity with a vessel's through-hull configuration, insufficient routine inspection regimes, and an absence of formalised pre-departure watertight integrity checks reflect systemic procedural shortcomings at the owner and operator level. Environmental conditions — particularly night operations and moderate sea states — reduce the crew's ability to detect ingress early, locate its source, or conduct effective damage control. The absence of operable VHF radio communications in several incidents further delayed distress alerting, demonstrating that multiple independent safeguards were either absent or had silently degraded prior to the emergency. Collectively, these incidents illustrate how the convergence of unmonitored hardware deterioration, procedural inattention, and environmental challenge transforms a preventable maintenance failure into a SAR-requiring emergency.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, and Watertight Integrity (including maintenance of hull openings, through-hull fittings, and bilge pumping arrangements)
SOLAS Chapter II-2: Fire Protection, Detection and Extinction (applicable by analogy to detection and alarm systems, including bilge alarm and monitoring requirements)
SOLAS Chapter IV: Radiocommunications (carriage and operability requirements for distress alerting equipment, including VHF DSC radio)
preventable_by: Mandate and enforce a structured pre-departure watertight integrity checklist for recreational and charter vessels that explicitly requires physical inspection and operation-testing of all through-hull fittings, seacocks, exhaust system connections, anti-siphon valves, and bilge pump functionality before each voyage, with records retained by the skipper or responsible person.

Require the installation and periodic testing of automatic bilge alarms and, where vessels are routinely left unattended at berth, remote monitoring systems capable of alerting the owner or a responsible party to rising bilge water levels in real time, thereby eliminating reliance on chance observation as the primary detection mechanism.

Introduce a mandatory operational readiness requirement for VHF radio carriage and crew competency on all vessels operating beyond sheltered waters at night or in reduced visibility, supported by targeted safety campaigns addressing exhaust system maintenance as a high-probability flooding pathway specific to auxiliary-engined sailing yachts.

representative_cases.description: A sailing yacht under engine started taking on water, as the bilges started to fill, the engine stopped and smoke was seen but no fire. The water ingress then stopped but both the bilges and engine bay were full, an anchor was deployed as the yacht was drifting towards a lee shore. The yacht requested assistance by mobile phone as there was no radio communication. A harbour vessel attended and tow

A charter fishing vessel was alongside, it was reported by members of the public that the vessel was sinking. A shore worker attended the scene as the vessel was unoccupied. The vessel was secured with additional mooring lines. There was minimal fuel aboard with no fuel seen leaking from the vessel, this was monitored and no boom defence deployed. The vessel was recovered four days later by a comm

A sailing yacht started taking on water, the pump was unable to cope with the ingress and the source of the ingress was unknown. A nearby vessel offered assistance until a search and rescue (SAR) vessel arrived and used their salvage pump. The SAR vessel then towed the sailing yacht to a harbour as the engine kept cutting out.

A small alluminium tender that was being used to conduct surveys in a shallow estuary began taking on water. The vessel was taken to a pontoon and tied up but continued to take on water and eventually became partially submerged. The vessel was towed across to the opposite side of the marina to where it was tied up and was lifted out by a crane and returned to its trailer.   There was no visible da

A pleasure craft began taking on water and called for assistance. A nearby search and rescue vessel was able to attend and provided a pump to control the water ingress. They then towed the vessel to a nearby peer and then back to its yacht club. All the crew were reported fit and well.

A stern trawler was found to be have a foot of water ingress inside the vessel's fish room coming out from the bottom of the vessel which appeared to to be a split. However it was able to make its own way into port utilising their own  hand held emergency pump that had been able to stem the water until the vessel arrived into port safely where it was also assisted by a lifeboat.

A sail training vessel was heading upwind when the bilge alarm went off so the bilges were pumped out. It was then reported that a floor board was floating, upon checking there was significant water in all of the bilges. The strainer was checked and each section pumped out but it started to fill again. The vessel turned around started to head back, a salvage pump set up and a PAN PAN issued. Two s

A fishing vessel reported water ingress in the engine room, the reason at the time was unknown. The vessel proceeded to the nearest harbour whilst the crew attempted to pump out the water. A nearby vessel escorted the fishing vessel until it made it safely to harbour where the water was pumped out by a search and rescue (SAR) vessel.

An offshore powerboat with two crew onboard started taking on water, two nearby vessel assisted along with two search and rescue (SAR) that deployed to the scene. Salvage pumps were placed onboard before the vessel was towed to safety. The water ingress was found to have come from the exhaust system.

Fishing vessel was taking on water which was followed by successful pumping actions. The crew members contacted the shore for assistance however, they were able to resolve the problem with the pumps. They realised that there was a 2 inch pipe where the leak was coming from. Crew then decided to turn the seacock off which stopped the ingression. The lifeboat escorted the vessel back to shore.

representative_cases.description: A cruise ship passenger was walking towards the open deck, as they exited the door on to the open deck the passenger slipped on the ramp falling to the deck. The passenger was treated for a fracture to the right wrist. The passenger was not medically disembarked. No defects were found at the scene.

The crew of a ro-pax vessel were working in the void space for the clam doors to clean up a hydraulic oil spill when one crew member slipped on the combination of hydraulic oil and degreaser and fell to the deck. They stopped work and were given first aid on board in the form of pain relief. However the injury did not improve and the crewmember disembarked and attended a local hospital where it wa

A ro-ro cargo crew member was descending a stair case when they slipped on a step and slid down the rest of the way to the bottom of the stairs. The crew member's right arm got caught on the hand rail as they were sliding down resulting in a dislocated shoulder. The crew member received medical treatment on board due to medically trained supernumeraries being on board at the time. The crew member

A service vessel crew member slipped on a door seal resulting in a sprained ankle. The injured party was leaving the walk-in fridge and stepped onto the door frame sill, when their right foot slipped. The injured party's right knee twisted and as they put weight onto their left foot, they then sprained their left ankle.

Class V passenger vessel guest slipped and suffered a suspected broken leg. Lifeboat crew gave initial casualty care before transferring to the care of the ambulance service who transferred the casualty to hospital.

Mooring launch crew member slipped on a newly painted surface on the gunwale whilst boarding resulting in a minor injury. The mooring launch was taken out of service and repainted with anti-slip material. The crew member continued to work after the fall.

A cruise ship passenger fell and broke their wrist. They were standing next to an x-ray machine when they slipped and in an effort to break their fall they put out their hand resulting in a broken wrist. The area was inspected and there were no defects found however the floor had been recently polished the night before. The passenger was wearing flat rubber soled shoes.

A cruise passenger slipped on wet tile resulting in a fracture to the face. Passenger exiting the buffet seating area aft going toward the elevators, right foot slipped on the marble tile next to the carpet causing the fall face forward. Medical report: Fracture of the nasal bridge.

A cruise ship passenger was entering the bathroom in their cabin when they stepped on the metal threshold with their foot and slipped. They managed to prevent their fall by grabbing the wash basin table and the bathtub, however their foot bent back causing a fracture. The scene of the accident was inspected and no obvious defects were found. There were no damages or pollution.

A cruise ship passenger was walking in the pool area when they slipped and fell, sustaining a fractured patella. The area was reported to be wet at the time due to rain and there were warning signs in place to alert passengers to the wet surface. The vessel reported that the wet floor and lack of care on the part of the passenger were what caused them to slip.

title: Mechanical Lifting System Failures Under Adverse Operational Conditions
description:
This theme is characterised by the failure or loss of control of mechanical lifting and transfer systems — including replenishment winches, lifeboat davits, and helicopter hoists — during operational or maintenance deployments, frequently occurring under degraded environmental conditions such as night-time, elevated sea states, and wind. A recurring pattern across these incidents is the convergence of undetected latent hardware defects with procedural shortfalls: crews apply improvised or temporary fixes rather than executing controlled abort procedures, maintenance regimes fail to identify developing mechanical faults before they manifest operationally, and contingency protocols for compound failure scenarios are either absent or inadequately rehearsed. The consequence is that personnel are routinely exposed to residual hazards — snap-back energy, prolonged immersion, or uncontrolled mechanical loads — that a well-structured emergency response should have mitigated or eliminated entirely.

Latent hardware deficiencies are a primary driver across this cluster, manifesting as brake failures, electrical burnouts, wire overloads, and undetected davit mechanism faults that escape detection through infrequent operational use and insufficiently rigorous maintenance programmes. These mechanical vulnerabilities are compounded by human factors, including inadequate crew knowledge of snap-back danger zones, poor situational awareness of emergency abort criteria, and decision-making under time pressure that prioritises continuity of operation over safety. Environmental conditions — particularly night-time reduced visibility and sea states that exceed formally assessed parameters — act as force multipliers, masking early mechanical warning signs and increasing dynamic loading on already-stressed systems. The systemic latent cause is an organisational tolerance of maintenance and training gaps in life-saving and transfer appliance systems, where the infrequency of failure creates a false assurance of reliability until conditions conspire to expose the underlying deficiencies.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (covering davit systems, lifeboat deployment, LSA maintenance and drills)
SOLAS Chapter VI / VII – Carriage of Cargoes and Dangerous Goods (covering replenishment and transfer operations, winch and hose-handling equipment)
SOLAS Chapter I – General (survey, inspection, and maintenance standards applicable to shipborne mechanical equipment and operational readiness)
preventable_by: Implement enhanced periodic operational testing and condition-based maintenance protocols for all mechanical lifting and transfer systems (winches, davits, hoist equipment), requiring documented pre-operation inspections before each deployment and independent verification of brake, wire, and limit-switch integrity — not solely reliance on scheduled drill cycles.

Develop and mandate vessel- and operation-specific emergency abort procedures for winch, davit, and hoist failures, including clearly defined snap-back exclusion zones, wire-tension management protocols, and coordinated contingency plans for multi-asset operations (e.g. helicopter–vessel coordination for winch failure scenarios), with competency assessed through realistic scenario-based drills.

Introduce mandatory pre-operation environmental threshold assessments that reconcile reported sea state classifications with actual observed wave heights and wind speeds, with defined operational limits that trigger automatic suspension or escalated risk controls when conditions exceed planned parameters — preventing operations from proceeding under formally under-assessed environmental conditions.

representative_cases.description: Replenishment tanker lost control of its winch wire which had tensioned between two ships, when a crew member managed to make a temporary fix, but it had the potential for a snap-back if the wire was to snap.

A service vessel lifeboat davit failed to operate during maintenance. .  As the davit was being deployed, it suddenly stopped at approx. 30 deg from the horizontal deck.  All attempts to continue to deploy or recover the davit were unsuccessful.

A service vessel was carrying out operations with a helicopter winching crewmembers to and from the deck. During one winching operation, the helicopter winch encountered difficulties wherein the person being winched was dropped onto the aft railings causing them to fall onto the transom of the vessel. To prevent the helicopter becoming entangled with the vessel, the crew of the helicopter cut the

A replenishment tanker was carrying out a replenishment activity whilst doing a transfer. This caused the winches controlling the transfer hose to experience electrical failure, The crew restored power by replacing the faulty electrical components which had burned out.

A replenishment tanker was carrying out an operation of the lifeboat davit using the ‘Dead Ship Mode’ (DSM) lowering without electrical power to the system. The first practice was with an unoccupied lifeboat under electrical power. The crew then embarked ready for lowering via the DSM method which is controlled from inside the boat. Once the lifeboat was in position for lowering, pressure was appl
Tanker was in passage when it experienced heavy weather conditions. As a result a lifeboat free fell and was lost.

A standby vessel was launching its fast rescue craft (FRC) as a test. The crew boarded the FRC, the davit deployed the vessel but the spooling winch also started causing the FRC to tilt. One crewmember was tossed into the water and was recovered by the FRC once it was in the water. The crewmember was wearing a lifejacket and sustained no injuries.  A valve within the davit launch system was found

During the recovery of the FRC (fast rescue craft) following a drill, with one crewmember onboard, the wire drum free fall break on the davit  failed and started lowering the boat back to sea level. This was from approximately one metre high. The crewmember exited the FRC at sea level via the embarkation door . The brake was tested several times and intermittently failed to hold the FRC in a suspe
A boat davit collapsed when carrying out a load test on a workboat davit. No pollution occurred.  There was structural damage to the starboard workboat aft davit arm.

Whilst alongside in harbour, routine maintenance was being carried out on a lifeboat.  The boat had been lowered and was being hoisted when it unexpectedly began to slowly lower itself.  The crew attempted to stop the descent manually but this had no effect.  The boat was empty at the time and there were no injuries to crew or damage to the boat, davit structure or fall wires. representative_cases.description: General cargo vessel suffered an engine failure so the crew decided to drop the anchor. The vessel touched the bottom on the port side. However, the vessel was refloated with the assistance from a local craft and was taken under tow to port.

A fishing vessel experienced a propulsion failure due to a defective propeller, an anchor was deployed but didn't hold, the started to drift close to shore, a nearby vessel was able to assist and prevent the vessel from grounding but unable to tow. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked and established a tow. The stricken vessel was towed to a harbour.

A fishing vessel with two crew onboard had a mechanical failure, the crew dropped an anchor but the anchor started to drag. Further attempts were made but the vessel continued to drift towards a rocky reef. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was deployed and towed the stricken vessel to a harbour.

A fishing vessel had an engine failure, the lone skipper put out an anchor whilst they attempted to restart the engine and send a MAYDAY via radio. Cargo vessel lost propulsion power when approaching the bridge and dropped both emergency anchors. The vessel managed to stop right before the bridge however, due to the wind the vessel was pushed and made contact with the corner of a pier. There were no damages on either the vessel or the pier.

A windfarm crew transfer vessel was manoeuvring in a port area when it experienced control failure and made contact with a pontoon. It then drifted across the harbour. The crew tried to stop the drift by dropping the anchor but it was not effective in preventing the vessel from colliding with a moored fishing vessel. Small electric engine not powerful enough to get out of the situation. 2 POB abandoned ship and swam 2 miles back to shore, vessel then drifted and ran around due to onshore winds, sails still in sail bags.

An offshore windfarm crew transfer vessel experienced propulsion failure shortly after departing from the berth and began to drift up river on the current. The crew noticed that the vessel was drifting onto a nearby ferry terminal and deployed an anchor however the anchor did not hold the vessel. representative_cases.description: A ro-pax vessel crew member was lifting a box of stores when they collapsed. When they regained consciousness it was discovered that they had dislocated their elbow.
The passenger ship was berthing once a crew member hurt their back due to pulling up the slack in the mooring line by hand.
A service vessel crewmember was moving a hose and when pressing it onto the manifold and twisting it, the crewmember experienced back pain.
A cruise ship crewmember suffered a sprained back while lifting a crate of cutlery. The crewmember was unable to continue their duties and was later medically disembarked.
Cruise ship's crew member was in the process to lift the upper bunk when they felt pain in their knee. Crew member disembarked as they required further medical assistance.
A cruise ship crewmember was moving a baggage trolley when they were unable to stop it and it struck their ankle resulting in a strained foot. The crewmember was signed off work for 7 day.
A replenishment tanker crew member was going down a ladder when they banged their head on a sprinkler and sustained a cut to their ear.
A crewmember on a ro-ro ferry strained their back whilst heaving in a line during departure from its mooring resulting in them being signed off work.
Passenger Ro-Ro's crew member tripped while carrying a dish resulting in open wound to the finger.
A crew transfer vessel windfarm technician injured their back lifting a personal bag awkwardly. The crewmember was met by an ambulance ashore.

representative_cases.description: General cargo vessel broke their anchor chain and lost one anchor. The anchor shackle snapped during anchor lifting and the owners decided that the cost to recover the anchor exceeded the value of the anchor.

A ro-pax vessel experienced water ingress into two forward void spaces. The vessel had just come alongside at its arrival port when the crew were alerted to bilge alarms in two of the forward void spaces. It is believed that the anchor was not housed correctly and caused holes in the hull resulting in water ingress. There was no pollution or injuries.

A ro-ro cargo vessel allegedly caused damage to an underwater cable with their anchor. The vessel was at anchor in a designated anchorage. They were contacted the next day by the company that manages the fibre optic cable in the area. The cable reportedly was damaged and the vessel had been anchored in that area at the time of the damage.

A sail training vessel was at anchor close to the coast when it began to drag anchor and grounded approximately 10m from the shore. The crew broadcast a mayday and the local lifeboat service dispatched a vessel to assist. Before the lifeboat arrived, the sail training vessel refloated and the crew were able to get it back underway. The lifeboat escorted the sail training vessel back to a nearby ma

A cruise ship was at anchor, the vessel was swinging on its anchor, with one main engine and two bow thrusters on line to alleviate the swing. One of the links in the anchor chain broke resulting in the loss of the port anchor and approximately one cable of chain. The vessel maintained position as the main engine and two bow thrusters were still online at the time of incident. There was no damage

The incident occurred while the vessel was lying to its port anchor in 35 to 40 knots of wind. One of the links in the anchor chain broke resulting in the loss of the anchor and three cables of chain. The anchor and chain were recovered at a later date.

SAR vessel stuck submerged unknown object resulting in damaging the propeller blades. Anchor fouled on submerged object or wreckage. During manoeuvres by lifeboat to free anchor, the starboard propeller struck part of the object below the surface. Propeller blades damaged beyond repair.  Anchor subsequently freed from object and lifeboat continued training exercise.

A general cargo vessel dragged anchor in adverse weather conditions and was close to grounding. The crew attempted to hold position using the engine but continued to drag closer to shore. Lifeboats and a rescue helicopter were initially tasked but were stood down once the vessel recovered under its own power to safer water. There was no reported damage or injuries.

A ro-ro passenger vessel accidentally released their anchor on approach to harbour. Approximately two cables ran out and the anchor was initially dragged along the sea bed. It transpired that the automatic release mechanism failed at speed and the entire port anchor cable was let go.

A cruise ship was at anchor in high winds when it started to drag the anchor. The crew started the engine and heaved up the anchor. They recovered the anchor cable, but the anchor itself was missing. The reason for this was thought to be a failed D shackle.

Waste disposal operators on board a passenger ship discovered a small fire within the waste silo. The vessels incinerator had been shutdown the morning prior and consequently meant that no damage had been caused to the silo - as far as could be seen on a visual inspection. The crew discharged a water and CO2 extinguishers as well as the silo's water mist system.

A trawler was alongside in port when a fire started on a tumble dryer. This produced a lot of smoke and the five crew disembarked from the vessel onto the quayside and the shore side fire service attended. One crew member was given first aid for smoke inhalation by the fire fighters who also removed the tumble dryer from the vessel. There was no serious damage, pollution or injuries.

Smoke was noticed in the galley on a cruise ship. The crew members proceeded to investigate when they noticed a fire in the oven. The crew extinguished the fire with two CO2 extinguishers. Discovered a small Fire from the dryer machine drum that was extinguished, by First Stage Response, using the closest dry Powder fire Extinguisher.

title: Solo Passage Failures: Redundancy, Competency, and Preparedness
description: This theme is characterised by the convergence of single-handed operation, critical system failures, and inadequate pre-departure risk management across a range of vessel types and passage profiles. In each incident, the absence of crew redundancy transforms an otherwise manageable technical or navigational problem — engine failure, tiller breakage, adverse conditions — into a situation requiring SAR intervention. The recurring pattern reveals a systemic failure at the pre-departure stage: skippers undertaking solo passages do not consistently demonstrate, nor are they consistently required to demonstrate, that their competency, vessel equipment, and emergency protocols are commensurate with the demands of the intended passage. When the primary system fails, there is no fallback — human, technical, or procedural — to prevent escalation, and SAR becomes the sole safeguard in the risk chain.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (emergency preparedness, distress signalling, and survival equipment requirements for vessels on offshore passages)
SOLAS Chapter IV – Radiocommunications (mandatory carriage and operability of distress and safety communications equipment, including redundant systems for vessels operating beyond coastal waters)

SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (voyage planning obligations, assessment of vessel and crew fitness for the intended passage, and the duty to carry appropriate navigational equipment including AIS)
preventable_by: Mandatory pre-departure competency and passage fitness assessments for solo offshore passages: harbour authorities, marina operators, and coastguard agencies should implement a structured gatekeeping function requiring lone skippers to demonstrate that their certified competency level, vessel condition, and onboard redundancy (emergency steering, backup propulsion, and communications) are proportionate to the planned passage, with particular scrutiny applied to night passages and exposed coastal routes.

Compulsory float plan registration and check-in protocols for single-handed voyages: all solo passages beyond defined coastal limits should require submission of a float plan to the relevant Maritime Rescue Co-ordination Centre (MRCC) or designated shore contact, including expected waypoints, check-in schedules, and escalation thresholds — with the absence of a scheduled check-in triggering a graduated SAR alert to reduce the overdue detection window from days to hours.

Regulatory requirement for minimum onboard redundancy standards on solo-crewed vessels: vessels undertaking single-handed passages should be required to carry and maintain in a ready-to-deploy state at least one redundant steering solution (emergency tiller, steering oar, or autopilot backup), one independent means of propulsion (outboard engine in deployable condition or auxiliary sail configuration), and a minimum of two independent distress communication devices (including a registered EPIRB and handheld VHF), with compliance verified as part of any applicable vessel safety examination scheme.

representative_cases.description: A sailing yacht reported an engine failure but had power. Radio communication between the lone skipper and the emergency services was broken so a search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to assist. The yacht was towed back to a marina and safely berthed.

A sailing yacht was on passage under engine when the yacht had an engine failure. The lone skipper continue under mainsail but was not an experienced sailor and struggled with the conditions. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was deployed to assist the yacht to ensure the skippers safety. The SAR vessel took the yacht under tow to a harbour. No injury to the skipper, no damage to vessel reported.

A sailing yacht broke its tiller, the lone skipper had no steering and was unable to get the outboard engine prepared before the yacht grounded at the entrance to a harbour. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to assist, the water was too shallow to tow the yacht clear so the skipper was transferred to the SAR vessel and taken ashore. As the yacht was not anchored, there was concern the ya

A singe handed sailing vessel was taking part in a challenge and was reported as overdue. The vessel had no AIS and no satellite. The vessel was overdue by ten days and then the boat was found empty by a passing fishing vessel. The RNLI towed the sailing vessel to harbour and the sailing skipper is presumed deceased.

A lone skipper onboard a fishing vessel requested assistance by radio, another vessel starting towing the fishing vessel. During the tow the fishing vessel had a shift in weight on-board, possibly crates of fish, the tow was released and an attempt to recover the skipper. The fishing vessel sank, an emergency call was placed with search and rescue (SAR) assets tasked to the scene.

The lifeboat was putting a crew member ashore to aid 3 swimmers who had been washed onto rocks, a larger wave picked up the lifeboat and then as the water receded it grounded onto what where once submerged rocks causing damage to the hull.

A solo sailor was sailing and making way to a harbour when they injured their hand whilst tacking. As they were unable to enter the harbour safely on their own the skipper requested assistance. A decision was made to task a search and rescue (SAR) vessel to assist as necessary. To SAR went onboard to assess the skippers injury and set up a tow. The yacht was towed to a mooring in the harbour, the
A sailing vessel tiller broke and the vessel lost steering. It then subsequently ran aground. The vessel was being operated single-handedly

A single handed potter skipper was taking their vessel from one port to another but were reported overdue after being out for more than ten hours. They were rescued by search and rescue services. They had become disorientated as it became dark and were succumbing to the effects of the cold, however they were not severely injured.

A pleasure sailing yacht was anchored next to a breakwater on a lee shore. The wind was building throughout the day and there was concern for the crew member, a search and rescue vessel was tasked to investigate and remove the crew member. They were unable to do so due to the proximity to the surf zone, the anchor eventually failed and the yacht drifted onto the breakwater and started to break up.

title: Navigational Misjudgement During High-Risk Confined Transits
description:
This theme is characterised by navigational errors occurring during high-risk, spatially constrained manoeuvres — principally bridge transits, platform approaches, and pilotage in restricted waters — where officers or pilots misjudge clearances, issue incorrect helm orders, or become distracted from their primary watchkeeping duties. A recurring pattern across incidents is the absence of structured procedural frameworks specific to these manoeuvres: no formal bridge transit protocols, no platform approach checklists, and no enforced helm order verification procedures. This procedural vacuum means that individual human error — whether a momentary lapse in situational awareness, a miscommunicated helm order, or distraction by an administrative task — is not intercepted by any systemic safeguard, allowing minor misjudgements to escalate into contacts, groundings, or crew injuries. The frequency of these incidents suggests an organisational tolerance for ad hoc navigation practices during phases that objectively demand the highest level of procedural discipline.

Latent hardware and environmental conditions consistently reduce the margin available to detect and recover from human error. Night-time operations appear as a recurring environmental factor, degrading visual situational awareness precisely when accurate perception of clearances and vessel positioning is most critical. Moderate wind and sea state further reduce manoeuvrability and helm responsiveness, compressing the time available for corrective action. Hardware deficiencies — including the absence of active ECDIS guard zones, proximity alarms, and adequate bow fendering — mean that vessels lack automated early-warning capability and physical resilience during close-quarters work. The combination of degraded environmental conditions, absent automation safeguards, and procedural gaps creates a predictable latent failure pathway: the officer or pilot is the sole, unsupported barrier against contact or grounding, and when that barrier fails, no secondary defence exists to prevent the incident or mitigate its consequences.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (particularly Regulations 15 and 34 concerning bridge procedures, passage planning, and safe watchkeeping practices)
SOLAS Chapter VIII – Watchkeeping (Standards for watchkeeping at sea and in port, including officer distraction and bridge resource management obligations)
SOLAS Chapter XI-2 / ISM Code (International Safety Management Code – obligation to establish safe working procedures, identify hazards, and implement corrective safeguards)
preventable_by: Mandate the development and use of formal High-Risk Manoeuvre Protocols for all bridge transits, platform approaches, and pilotage operations, requiring a pre-manoeuvre briefing, defined roles for all bridge team members, a helm order repeat-back and verification procedure, and prohibition of non-navigational tasks for the duration of the manoeuvre phase.

Require active use of ECDIS guard zones and proximity alarm systems during all confined-water and close-quarters approaches, with standing orders that automated alerts must be acknowledged and acted upon before the manoeuvre commences, providing a hardware-based secondary barrier independent of officer vigilance.

Implement a vessel-wide movement warning system — analogous to an aviation seat-belt sign — requiring the officer of the watch to broadcast an all-crew alert prior to any high-risk manoeuvre so that crew members in unsecured working positions can take appropriate precautions, directly addressing the latent risk of crew injury from contact events during confined-water transits.

representative_cases.description: A utility vessel made contact with a concrete support of a bridge resulting in minor damage and one minor injury. There was a dent in the bow roller, no damage to the ships hull. Due to the impact, an AB working in the galley lost his balance and fell in the entrance to the accommodation below deck and injured his head.

A general cargo vessel grounded on shingle and was stuck for ten minutes before returning to port. The grounding was reportedly due to an incorrect helm order from the pilot which meant the vessel failed to turn as intended.

The vessel's second mate had just taken over the watch. The was no current work available to do so the vessel was on normal standby and was required to carry out cargo operations at a local platform. Later during the watch, the second mate started towards the platform at a slow speed. At approximately 4.5 knots, the 2nd mate then started to carry out work on the bridge computer and stopped maintai

Cargo vessel's crew was informed that the bridge was fully open when they proceeded and adjusted the speed as necessary. The vessel made light contact with the cabin structure of the bridge resulting in minor paint damage. The berthing was proceeded and no other damages were reported.

A passenger ship's ramp was raised whilst passengers were disembarking resulting in passengers having to jump onto the slipway. The crew held the ramp in position until the passengers cleared the area and then offered support and first aid to the passengers. One passenger sustained a minor injury. The incident occurred to a reported 'communication break down' between the bridge and the deck becaus

An offshore supply vessel was shifting from one berth to another. The master was manoeuvring the vessel past a Ro/Ro passenger ferry when the officer supporting the master provided the wrong distance. The master applied power on the port engine and the distance was not checked from the starboard quarter to the passenger ferry. This resulted in the supply vessel making contact with the bow of the f

A general cargo vessel was turning inside a dock area when the bow of the vessel struck the quay. The vessel was under pilotage at the time. There were no structural damages to the quay and slight damage to the bow and forward railings of the vessel. The incident reportedly happened due to 'pilot error'. The vessel reported that they should have been critical of the pilot to prevent the accident (

An inland waterways passenger vessel made contact with a bridge, resulting in minor damage to the wheelhouse and also to a fitting on the bridge. There was no structural damage to the bridge and no material damage to the vessel. There were no injuries or pollution. The vessel was able to move away from the bridge under its own power and proceed to a berth.

The vessel made contact with a bridge buttress, resulting in damage to the bow above the water line which consisted of 60cm of splits and 260cm in length with a spread of 25cm. The vessel successfully managed to anchor, and no injuries to any persons onboard were confirmed. Damage to the bridge buttress wooden fendering and concrete top face.

An offshore windfarm crew supply vessel was drifting after deploying some teams to offshore turbines. The master was reportedly distracted by paperwork and didn't notice that the vessel was on a collision course with a turbine. The vessel hit the turbine at 5kts. A crewmember was exiting the toilet at the time of the impact causing them to fall and injure their ribs (fracture) The vessel returned

title: Mobility Aid Misuse and Passenger Vulnerability Risks Aboard
description:
This theme is characterised by a convergence of improvised passenger behaviour, unregulated mobility aid operation, and the absence of structured vulnerability assessment processes. Incidents in this cluster recur because vessels lack systematic mechanisms to identify mobility-impaired or at-risk passengers and to translate that identification into targeted supervision, physical safeguards, or behavioural guidance. Companions and passengers themselves routinely improvise solutions to mobility challenges — pushing walkers as wheelchairs, operating scooters in restricted areas, or performing routine movements without seeking assistance — in ways that are foreseeable yet unaddressed by any formal shipboard procedure or environmental control. The shipboard setting amplifies these risks through sloped decks, abrupt flooring transitions, open deck edges, and vessel movement, converting low-consequence shore-side behaviours into serious or fatal injury events.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including wheeled mobility aids without effective braking, insufficient physical barriers at open deck edges, and abrupt mixed-surface flooring transitions — create an environment in which a single lapse of control or judgement produces disproportionate consequences. Human factors, including underestimation of personal risk, unfamiliarity with vessel dynamics, and the instinctive desire of companions to assist without formal guidance, sustain a pattern of unsafe improvised behaviour. Environmental conditions such as night-time operation, deck slope, and vessel movement while manoeuvring compound these vulnerabilities. Critically, the absence of passenger-facing safety information, access controls to safety-critical spaces, and crew training in mobility aid risk management constitutes a systemic latent failure within the Safety Management System that permits these foreseeable incidents to occur repeatedly across diverse vessel types.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (structural deck design, flooring transitions, and physical barriers)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (passenger safety briefings, vulnerability identification, and evacuation provisions for mobility-impaired persons)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code – Safety Management System obligations including risk assessment, procedures, and passenger safety controls)
preventable_by: Implement a formal passenger vulnerability identification process at embarkation, requiring crew to flag and record mobility-impaired or at-risk passengers, assign dedicated safety contacts, and provide targeted briefings on the safe use of mobility aids within the shipboard environment, including restrictions on areas such as the wheelhouse and guidance on slope and flooring hazards.

Establish and enforce access control policies for safety-critical and high-risk areas — including wheelhouses, open deck edges, and areas with abrupt flooring transitions — through physical barriers, signage, and coded procedural requirements that explicitly address powered and non-powered passenger mobility aids, including mandatory parking and restraint provisions when aids are left unattended.

Conduct a vessel-specific risk assessment of all passenger-accessible areas to identify and remediate environmental hazards relevant to mobility aid use, including installing transition strips or bevelled edges at mixed-surface flooring junctions, fitting guard chains or barriers of adequate height and strength at open deck edges, and displaying multilingual safety guidance on mobility aid use at lift lobbies, alleyways, and boarding points.

representative_cases.description: A cruise ship passenger was with their spouse and was sitting on their mobility aid walker in the alleyway/lobby area near the elevators. Their spouse attempted to push the walker away from the elevator whilst the injured passenger was still sitting on it. The deck there had a slight slope and the walker was fitted with wheels, which lead to the walker moving faster than expected before getting ca

An inland passenger vessels crew member was struck by a passenger mobility scooter, resulting in superficial injuries. Passenger lost control of there mobility scooter and accelerated across the wheelhouse, pinning the skipper against the guard chain on the open port side, bruising both of the skipper’s shins and causing a small cut on the left wrist.

A passenger on board a paddle steamer was leaning down to tie their shoelace when they fell over and fractured their femur. The vessel was on its way to a berth and the local search and rescue vessel crew attended to evacuate the person ashore. It was reported that the passenger had balance issues more generally

A cruise ship passenger was walking in an alleyway towards the passenger lift when they reportedly tripped over their own feet resulting in a fall. When they fell, they extended their hands which impacted the floor resulting in a broken wrist on one side. The area was inspected and no defects were found that could've contributed to the fall. The passenger reported that they were unsteady on their

A ro-pax crew member was tasked to take on an assistance duty when they were at the bottom of stairs and were tasked with moving an empty passenger’s wheelchair from bottom of stairs to the top. From the vehicle deck to cabin up two flights of stairs, up one deck when the injured crew member and one other person went to pick up the chair, the hand hold the other crew member was using came free. As

A cruise ship passenger was entering their cabin when they fell onto the floor hitting their head and hip. They sustained fracture injuries. They were using a mobility aid at the time as they had poor balance. The area was inspected and nothing out of the ordinary was found.

A cruise ship passenger dislocated their knee whilst attempting to remove their footwear. They were in their cabin and balanced on one leg with the other leg resting across it. This put more pressure on the right knee than it could take, causing it to dislocate. The passenger reported that they had a knee replacement previously which could have lead to the knee becoming weaker. The area was inspec

A cruise ship passenger fell inside a tender whilst descending a set of steps. They fell onto their right side and sustained a fractured rib. It was reported that the incident occurred because the tender moved suddenly and that the passenger also had limited mobility.

The passenger was moving around their cabin. They lost their balance and fell, dislocating their artificial hip joint. No one witnessed the event. Their spouse found them less than 5 minutes later. The passenger had a history of mobility issues and had limited feeling in their legs.

A cruise ship passenger was walking in the restaurant area on board when they fell and fractured their shoulder. They used crutches to aid them in walking and one of their crutches hit their foot causing them to overbalance and fall to the floor. They were diagnosed with a fractured shoulder.

Critically, these deficiencies were frequently known or foreseeable prior to the manoeuvre, yet operations proceeded without effective compensatory measures. Manoeuvred the vessel port side too the supply vessel, unfortunately as the vessels came together the supply vessel lifted on a wave, SAR vessel was in the trough and when coming up the port handrails got stuck under the ships fendering, ripping off most of the Port stern rails of SAR

A ro-pax vessel was berthing at a terminal when it made contact with a fender, the fender rotated which punctured the vessel's shell plating. The damage was 45 meters or so from the bow, 5m approx. above the waterline, dimensions of the puncture being 50mm x 120mm approx. The fender sustained minor damage. The vessel was permitted to sail after completing temporary repairs that were approved by cl

A landing vessel was alongside the quay berthing with a pilot on board when it made contact with the quay. A fender was close to the mid-ships area causing the vessel to rotate under the wind conditions and the port quarter to impact the quay. The concrete jetty sustained minor damage, the vessel was undamaged and there were no injuries or pollution.

A product tanker was being berthed when its railings made contact with the quay. There was no damage to the railings. The contact was caused by the fact the vessel was laden combined with the height of tide meaning the railings were below the fenders. The vessel was re-positioned with the assistance of tugs.

A service vessel made contact with dock following loss of fender, resulting in minor paint damage. Vessel jolted, crew investigated and confirmed vessel in contact with dock due to missing fender. New fender put in place and all line secured.

General cargo was approaching a jetty to make an ebb approach. Whilst the vessel was backing into the lock park, some of the structure rubbed against the timber fendering causing it to slinter off more and damage the vessel on deck. This damage is damage on top of existing damage. No injuries and/or other damages reported.
A crew transfer vessel was alongside in a port when the fenders between it and the quay popped. This caused the hull of the vessel to come contact with the quay causing damage above the waterline.

As the passenger vessel was departing, a fender got caught on the shell door of the cruise ship, causing damage to the aft deck railings of the passenger vessel. Unable to manoeuvre, the passenger vessel came to a stop and a cleat on the bridge caused superficial paint damage to the cruise ship.

title: Cross-Jurisdictional Reporting Gaps Obscuring Safety Intelligence
description:
This theme is characterised not by a discrete physical marine safety event, but by a systemic failure in incident data governance — specifically, the loss of actionable safety information when investigation responsibility is transferred to another state authority. Across all incidents in this cluster, the originating state's reporting database retains only jurisdictional metadata, with no substantive detail on vessel type, incident category, causal factors, personnel involvement, or outcomes. Compounding this, multiple records are confirmed duplicates of existing reports, indicating weaknesses in input validation and reporting workflow controls. The result is a corpus of serious and very serious incidents — some occurring in operationally significant environmental conditions including night-time operations, high winds, and significant wave heights — that are entirely invisible to national-level safety trend analysis. The recurrence of this pattern across a substantial number of records suggests the problem is structural rather than incidental.

From a latent causation perspective, the primary drivers are organisational and systemic rather than hardware or human error in an operational sense. The absence of a standardised inter-jurisdictional data-sharing framework means that when investigation authority is delegated, the originating state has no formalised mechanism to retrieve or integrate the receiving authority's findings. Data quality issues are further evident in multiple records where wave height values are inconsistent with recorded sea state classifications, suggesting either sensor faults, manual data entry errors, or auto-populated metadata that has not been validated. Human factors manifest as procedural non-compliance in report submission, including the repeated logging of duplicate entries, pointing to insufficient user training and absent system-level deduplication safeguards. While environmental conditions — including night operations and moderate-to-rough sea states — are present across several records and may have been operationally relevant, they cannot be assessed as contributing factors without the underlying incident narratives. The cumulative effect is a material degradation of the national safety intelligence picture, with serious and very serious incidents effectively disappearing from the evidence base used to inform risk management and policy.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter I – General Provisions (Regulation 21: Casualty investigation obligations and flag state reporting duties)
SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code: Safety management system documentation, reporting, and cross-authority communication requirements)
SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulation 31-32: Reporting of dangers and casualty reporting obligations to flag and port state authorities)
preventable_by: Establish a mandatory inter-jurisdictional incident data-sharing protocol requiring investigating state authorities to transmit full investigation findings, causal classifications, and outcome data to the originating state's safety database within a defined timeframe, ensuring no serious or very serious incident is recorded without substantive narrative detail.

Implement automated deduplication and data completeness validation controls within the incident reporting system, including mandatory field requirements for incident category, vessel type, and causal factors before a report can be submitted, and system-level flagging or rejection of entries sharing identical incident reference numbers.

Introduce a periodic data quality audit process whereby all records classified as 'investigated by another state' or flagged as duplicates are reviewed by a designated data governance officer, cross-referenced with the responsible authority's findings, and either enriched with complete investigation data or formally archived — with audit outcomes reported annually to support national safety trend analysis.

A service vessel was taking part in an exercise. A man overboard alarm had been activated, crew responded to the alarm and proceeded to the upper decks. A crew member was transiting across the deck to the other side of the vessel when they  slipped on ice and landed on the right knee and dislocated it. Further investigation revealed that the man overboard alarm had been set off accidently, but was

They had had crossed the threshold and were inside the changing room, when they slipped on a small pool of water on the deck. They then received first aid by other crew members, they were taken off the vessel in order to transfer them because an x ray was required at a local hospital.

A tug was alongside a berth and was making fast. A crew member was on the quayside putting mooring line on the bollards. As the crew member returned to board the vessel, they slipped on an icy patch as they approached the boarding ladder. The crew member fell and injured their shoulder on impact.  The crew member boarded but as the day progressed it became apparent the injury was more serious. The

Sailing vessel was in passage when the casualty slipped and got their foot between the starboard wheel and the pedestal which resulted in a closed hip fracture. The individual will make a full recovery. No defects were found in the area.

A crewmember was working on board a passenger vessel whilst it was alongside when they slipped on the deck and fell overboard. Help was provided by a nearby vessel also alongside. The crewmember was not wearing a lifejacket and they were recovered uninjured.

The vessel was alongside in a  berth,  a crew member slipped whilst walking across deck, banged their head and began fitting. The crew member was taken via ambulance, to hospital where they stayed overnight and  to remained for observations until at least 3 days. They were diagnosed with a fracture to the head

Two crew members were working on deck preparing the pilot ladder for use. The vessel was alongside but they were experiencing bad weather with heavy rainfall. When unfurling the ladder, a crewmember slipped and fell on their back. After having the injury looked at, they returned to work soon after but their ankle was uncomfortable and it was later discovered after an X-ray that the ankle was broke
