representative_cases.description: An inland waterways passenger vessel was on a sight-seeing voyage on a river when it experienced propulsion failure. The vessel's skipper dropped anchor and notified the operations and maintenance team of the operating company to send assistance. The passengers were transferred to another inshore passenger vessel and brought ashore. The maintenance team established that the bolts had snapped on a

A service vessel was on passage when it lost power from a suspected gear box failure. Two nearby vessels responded and proceeded to the scene. A tow was established and the service vessel towed to a harbour. No reported injuries or vessel damage.

A fisheries patrol vessel was underway when the engine made a grinding noise and emitted smoke and steam. The crew stopped the engine and investigated finding oil and coolant in the bilges. The crew found debris and a hole in the engine block. The cause of the propulsion failure was conrod failure. The vessel was towed into harbour for repairs.

Pilot boat experienced an issue with the port engine which led to increased noise and vibration levels. Crew members reported that the audiometer levels had increased. The vessel was taken out of service and after an investigation by a contractor, it was found that the gearbox bearings were worn.

A general cargo vessel was transiting through a canal whilst under pilotage when the lubricating oil system for the main engine suffered a cracked pipe. This resulted in lubricating oil leaking out into the engine room and falling below the minimum required level for ME operation. The ME was stopped and carried out an emergency anchoring procedure under the pilot's guidance. The vessel then berthe

A search and rescue vessel was engaged in a training exercise when it experienced propulsion failure due to a faulty gearbox. The vessel was towed back to port by another search and rescue vessel. There were no damages, injuries or pollution.

A service vessel's hooped cage around a vertical ladder sheared off and fell to the deck, causing minor damage to the deck. The steel bar experienced weld failure due to high levels of vibration in the area.

A fishing vessel was on passage when it started having an oil pressure issue in the gearbox and started to overheat. This resulted in the vessel having to reduce speed, due to the deteriorating conditions a vessel was arranged to tow the fishing vessel to a port.

After departure from port, en-route to an offshore survey site, a fire alarm was triggered from the steering room and tween deck of a service ship. Some smoke was also sighted at this time near the controlled pitch propeller (CPP). Upon inspection, it was observed that the smoke was coming from CPP housing following which the engine was stopped immediately for further assessment. Upon inspection,

A hopper dredger had a small engine fire after the starboard engine gearbox blew out spraying the engine room in oil. The fire was extinguished using a foam trolley extinguisher. The vessel anchored overnight and arranged to head to port for repair. No persons were injured.

title: Systematic Reporting Failures Under PCF Data Restrictions
description:
This theme is characterised not by a specific operational incident type, but by a pervasive and systemic failure in marine incident reporting, wherein all 17 records contain no substantive incident narrative beyond an administrative reference to a Permanent Cooperation Framework (PCF). The incidents span a range of severity classifications — from Less Serious to Very Serious — yet none provide the minimum data required for causal analysis, corrective action, or SOLAS compliance mapping. Recurring data quality anomalies compound this problem: multiple records exhibit significant discrepancies between recorded wave heights and reported sea state classifications, suggesting concurrent failures in environmental data integrity. Taken together, these records represent a structural breakdown in the safety reporting mechanism itself, which obstructs trend identification, regulatory oversight, and the prevention of future casualties. The recurrence of this pattern across 17 records indicates that the PCF reporting restriction is being applied systematically rather than exceptionally, and that no compensatory mechanism exists to recover or substitute the withheld incident data.

From a latent causal perspective, the primary failure mode is organisational and procedural rather than hardware or human-operational. The PCF administrative framework appears to be functioning as a barrier to transparent safety reporting, whether through deliberate data-sharing restrictions, ambiguous inter-authority protocols, or inadequate guidance to reporting officers on minimum narrative requirements. Human factors are evident in the repeated acceptance of incomplete submissions without escalation or rejection, suggesting a lack of data quality gatekeeping at the point of entry. Environmental conditions recorded across this cluster range from benign to genuinely adverse — including multiple nighttime operations with winds exceeding 40 kph and wave heights above 3 metres — meaning that for at least a subset of these incidents, environmental risk was materially elevated and the absence of an incident narrative represents a significant safety intelligence gap. The sea state classification discrepancies further indicate either observer error, sensor malfunction, or transcription failures that have gone uncorrected, pointing to broader deficiencies in data validation and reporting culture within the framework.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter I – General Provisions (Regulation 21: Casualty reporting obligations and flag State investigation requirements)
SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code: reporting, investigation, and corrective action under SMS obligations)
SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulation 31–32: Danger messages and mandatory hazard reporting duties)
preventable_by: Establish and enforce minimum mandatory data standards for all PCF-linked incident submissions, requiring a substantive incident narrative, validated sea state data, and vessel activity description before a report is accepted into the safety database; incomplete submissions must be automatically rejected and returned to the submitting authority with a defined resubmission deadline.

Implement a bilateral or multilateral PCF data recovery protocol that designates a named competent authority responsible for retrieving full incident details within 30 days of initial submission, with escalation procedures to flag State administrations and IMO if the deadline is not met, ensuring reporting restrictions do not permanently suppress safety-critical information.

Introduce automated data validation logic within the incident reporting system to detect and flag inconsistencies between recorded wave height and reported sea state classification at the point of entry, triggering mandatory reviewer verification before the record is finalised, thereby reducing compounding data quality errors that further undermine analytical value.

{
  "title": "Propeller Contact Due to Inadequate Underkeel Clearance Management",
  "description": "This theme encompasses incidents in which propulsion systems — including conventional propellers, Voith Schneider units, and outboard engines — make contact with the seabed, submerged objects, or launch infrastructure as a direct consequence of insufficient underkeel clearance. The incidents share a consistent pattern: vessels are committed to manoeuvres or launch sequences in water depths that are marginal or dynamically variable, without the benefit of formalised procedures that account for tidal state, sea conditions, vessel-specific propulsion geometry, or environmental visibility. Whether during routine channel transits, carriage launches, or positioning manoeuvres, the common thread is the absence of a structured go/no-go decision framework that would compel operators to evaluate depth adequacy before entering a risk-critical phase of operation. Night-time conditions compound this vulnerability in multiple cases by degrading the crew's ability to visually assess water depth, detect hazards, or monitor vessel behaviour during deployment.\n\nThe latent conditions driving this theme are systemic rather than incidental. From a hardware perspective, certain propulsion configurations — most notably Voith Schneider units, trimmed outboards, and propellers on shallow-drafted SAR vessels — present exposed profiles with limited ground clearance that are poorly accounted for in generic passage planning or launch protocols. Human factors manifest primarily as over-reliance on individual judgement in the absence of procedural constraints, with operators implicitly assuming acceptable conditions based on familiarity or routine rather than quantified assessment. Environmental factors, including tidal variation, wave-induced squat, swell under the stern, and discrepancies between reported and actual sea state, introduce dynamic underkeel clearance reductions that static pre-departure assessments fail to capture. The convergence of these latent conditions — inadequate vessel-specific procedures, propulsion geometry vulnerability, and dynamic environmental loading — creates the recurring scenario in which contact occurs at the moment of greatest exposure, when the vessel transitions from a controlled environment into open water or navigates a constrained, shallow channel.",

This did not impact the operation and no injuries were reported.

A search and rescue (SAR) vessel had trimmed its engine up in shallow water however a swell came underneath the vessel and caused the stern to drop down and the engine to ground. The vessel sustained damage to its propeller.

However between wave troughs the propeller came into contact with the sea bed causing damage to a propeller. No injuries to crew.

A search and rescue vessel was launching during a low tide (approximately 0.2m in height) when the propeller struck a submerged object. title: Chemical Exposure Injuries from Inadequate Hazard Controls
description:
This theme is characterised by crew injuries resulting from chemical exposure — including toxic gas inhalation, chemical burns, eye injuries from spray-back or splash, and product failure — occurring during routine operational or recreational activities aboard fishing vessels, Ro-Ro cargo ships, and service vessels. A consistent pattern emerges in which the handling of hazardous chemical substances proceeds without formal risk assessment, appropriate PPE, or procedural safeguards. Incidents recur because organisational safety cultures permit shortcuts under time pressure or operational routine, deviations from standard equipment and procedures are accepted without management of change processes, and crew hazard awareness training specific to chemical substances remains insufficient. The presence of night-time conditions across multiple incidents amplifies underlying vulnerabilities by reducing visibility, situational awareness, and supervisory oversight, converting nominally low-risk tasks into injury events.

Latent hardware and environmental factors consistently compound procedural and human failures in this theme. Non-standard or defective equipment — including unfamiliar sample bottles, defective snaplights, and unwieldy fuel containers — introduces unexpected failure modes that crews are not prepared to manage. Environmental conditions, particularly adverse sea states, vessel motion, darkness, and confined working spaces, reduce the margin for safe manual handling and increase exposure when chemical containment fails. Human factors including over-familiarity with routine tasks, insufficient training on chemical hazard recognition, and absence of stop-work authority further erode defences. Critically, the absence of engineered controls — gas detection alarms, mandatory PPE enforcement points, formal pre-task briefings, and management of change procedures for third-party equipment — means that individual crew judgement becomes the last and only barrier, an inherently unreliable safeguard in dynamic shipboard environments.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-2: Construction – Fire Protection, Detection and Extinction (including safe handling of hazardous substances and gas detection requirements)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (emergency response capability, MEDIVAC preparedness, and onboard medical provision)
SOLAS Chapter VI: Carriage of Cargoes (safe handling procedures for hazardous materials including fuels and chemicals used in cargo operations)
preventable_by: Implement mandatory pre-task risk assessments and formal permit-to-work controls for all operations involving hazardous chemical substances, including routine maintenance, fuel sampling, and gas-generating systems, with explicit requirements for appropriate PPE — including eye protection and respiratory protection — to be worn before work commences and verified by a responsible officer.

Establish and enforce a vessel-level Management of Change (MOC) procedure requiring documented risk assessment and supervisory approval before any deviation from standard equipment, materials, or operating procedures is accepted, including third-party-supplied equipment, to prevent ad hoc acceptance of unfamiliar items without compensatory controls.

Deliver targeted chemical hazard awareness training to all crew, including recognition of hazardous substances encountered in shipboard environments (ozone, rust cleaners, fuel, chemical light products), safe handling techniques, emergency first aid response to chemical exposure, and the boundaries of onboard medical capability, ensuring crew understand when and how to escalate to external medical assistance.

representative_cases.description: 2 engineers on a fish carrier inhaled ozone gas. They reported coughing and chest pain. They were provided with medical assistance, prior to that - they relaxed on fresh air.
A fishing vessel's crew member sustain eye injury from a chemical. Casualty sustained a serious eye injury from chemical, MEDIVAC provided from SAR.
Ro-Ro cargo ship's crew member suffered chemical burns from industrial rust cleaner. Crew member was taken for further medical advice.

a crew member proceeded to take three samples of Marine Gas Oil (MGO). Under normal procedures, supplied sample bottles are used for this task but the receiving vessel requested that the sample be taken using their own sample bottle. The bottle provided was of a different design from the standard sample bottles. While sampling, the crew member attempted to collect the MGO using the unfamiliar bott

Crew members on a service vessel had started to decorate a mess room for Christmas. It was decided to use bio luminescent snaplights as extra lighting for the tree. On breaking one of the snaplights to activate, it exploded and covered two crew members with the chemical substance. One of the crew members got some of the chemical in their left eye, their eye was flushed thoroughly with saline eye w

A service vessel crew member was decanting fuel from fuel bags into jerry cans. The crew member lifted a full jerry can to put drizet on the floor to prevent spillage. When the crew member placed the jerry can on the floor, fuel splashed out and into their eye. The crew member was treated immediately and returned to duties afterwards.

A fishing vessel crewmember was intending to clean the deck while the vessel was on passage and poured a chemical acid (Hypochlorite) into a container. The vessel rolled spilling the acid from the container and splashing it in to their eye. The crewmember immediately got into the shower and was then transported to hospital for treatment to flush out the acid.
A tanker crew member was exposed to several chemical products after their eyes were splashed.

A beam trawler crew member was injured by inhaling fumes from bleach. This resulted in lung irritation, coughing and an abnormal heart rhythm. The crew member wanted to remain on the vessel but their situation was bad enough that they were evacuated ashore for medical treatment, where the shore side ambulance service found an abnormal heart beat as well after they inhaled the fumes.

A service vessel crew member was placing an open tin of white spirit on to the deck, the liquid splashed out and into their eye. The crew member was treated immediately and returned to duties afterwards.

title: Engine Failure and Deficient Emergency Preparedness in Small Vessels
description:
This theme is characterised by propulsion system failures aboard small recreational and commercial motor vessels that escalate into search and rescue incidents due to an absence of effective emergency response measures, inadequate safety equipment, and poor pre-departure preparation. Across these incidents, engine failure — whether caused by mechanical fault, water ingress, fuel issues, or deferred maintenance — consistently serves as the proximate trigger. However, the recurring and preventable nature of these incidents points to deeper systemic deficiencies: crews departing without mandatory safety equipment including lifejackets and functioning communication devices, no redundant propulsion or anchoring capability, and an apparent absence of formalised pre-departure safety checks. The pattern of non-compliance with basic recreational and commercial vessel safety requirements indicates a broader safety culture deficit in which established maritime protocols are neither consistently enforced nor internalised by vessel operators.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including poorly maintained outboard engines, water ingress into engine compartments, and absent or unverified anchoring equipment — remove the vessel's primary means of propulsion and station-keeping at the moment they are most needed. These mechanical vulnerabilities are compounded by human factors including low situational awareness, absence of emergency drill preparedness, and inadequate crew medical risk management, which collectively prevent effective mitigation once a failure occurs. Environmental conditions, particularly night-time operations and moderate sea states, further reduce the margin for safe self-rescue and increase the complexity of SAR response. The convergence of these latent hardware failures, procedural non-compliance, and environmental exposure transforms individually manageable deficiencies into multi-agency SAR events, and in more adverse conditions could readily result in loss of life.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (requirements for lifejackets, distress signals, and survival equipment carriage and readiness)
SOLAS Chapter IV – Radiocommunications (mandatory carriage and operability of VHF radio and distress communication equipment)
SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (safe manning, voyage planning, pre-departure checks, and SAR coordination obligations)
preventable_by: Mandate and enforce structured pre-departure safety checklists for all recreational and small commercial vessels, verified through targeted compliance inspections by maritime authorities, covering lifejacket carriage and wear, functioning communication equipment, anchor readiness, and documented engine maintenance records.

Require vessel operators and crew to complete accredited short-course emergency preparedness training covering engine failure drills, anchor deployment, distress signalling procedures, and basic crew medical risk assessment, with particular emphasis on night-time and adverse-condition operations.

Introduce a programme of engine and vessel seaworthiness inspection for high-frequency recreational vessel users, combined with a public awareness campaign reinforcing the legal obligations for safety equipment carriage, the risks of operating without redundant propulsion or communication devices, and the personal and financial consequences of requiring preventable SAR intervention.

title: Recreational Activity Injuries Aboard Passenger Vessels at Night
description:
This theme encompasses injuries sustained by cruise ship passengers during onboard recreational activities — principally court sports such as basketball and table tennis, and social activities such as dancing — occurring predominantly under night-time conditions. The cluster is characterised by a convergence of dynamic physical activity, reduced environmental awareness, and an absence of formalised risk management frameworks governing passenger leisure spaces. Incidents recur because cruise operators treat recreational areas as amenity spaces rather than operationally managed risk environments, resulting in the absence of sea-state-dependent activity restrictions, inadequate pre-participation safety briefings, and limited supervision. The inherently dynamic nature of court sports and social dancing introduces foreseeable fall and collision hazards that are materially elevated aboard a moving vessel, yet procedural controls commensurate with that elevated risk are consistently absent across the incidents examined.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including unverified non-slip deck surfaces, the placement of obstructions such as speakers within or adjacent to activity areas, and the adequacy of perimeter barriers and fall-arrest features — represent a persistent physical risk layer that is rarely formally assessed. Human factors, including diminished spatial awareness and degraded reaction capability under night-time lighting, compounded by the close physical proximity inherent to multi-player sports, consistently elevate injury probability beyond what would be expected in equivalent onshore environments. Environmental conditions, particularly sea states ranging from slight to rough, introduce vessel motion that further compromises player balance and stability on recreational courts. Taken together, these latent factors illustrate a systemic failure to apply structured safety oversight to passenger recreational activities, with operators relying on passive acceptance of risk rather than proactive hazard mitigation — a gap that has resulted in injuries ranging from ligament ruptures and fractures to, in one case, a fatality following a fall on the dance floor.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision and Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (structural integrity and safety of spaces accessible to passengers)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (passenger safety briefings and emergency preparedness obligations)

SOLAS Chapter XII / ISM Code (International Safety Management): Safety Management Systems — risk assessment, operational procedures, and the duty to identify and control foreseeable hazards in passenger areas
preventable_by: Establish and enforce formal sea-state activity restrictions for all onboard recreational court sports and social dance venues, with clear operational thresholds (e.g., suspension of activities above Douglas Scale 3 or equivalent vessel motion criteria) documented within the vessel's Safety Management System and communicated to passengers via posted signage and daily activity briefings.

Mandate pre-participation safety briefings for all organised and self-directed recreational sport activities, covering appropriate footwear requirements, collision-avoidance conduct, fall risk awareness under vessel motion, and night-time visibility limitations; record passenger acknowledgement and ensure crew supervision is rostered for all night-time recreational facility operations.

Conduct periodic formal risk assessments of all passenger recreational areas covering deck surface non-slip compliance, adequate and uniform lighting levels for night-time use, the placement and securing of all equipment and fixtures (including speakers, tables, and court furniture) to eliminate trip and collision hazards, and the provision of appropriate perimeter barriers or cushioned fall-arrest features around active play areas.

The injured passenger was disembarked at the port, taken to a local hospital, and was diagnosed with a fractured right hip.

Cruise ship's passenger was playing basketball when they moved forward with the ball and ran into another player. This resulted in the passenger falling and sustaining a fractured wrist. No defects were found in the area.

A cruise ship passenger tripped over a speaker on the dance floor leading to a fractured humerus. They were taken to a local hospital for treatment and discharged as fir to re-join the vessel. However, six days later after signing off the vessel at the final destination they died in hospital.

A cruise ship passenger was playing table tennis when they rolled over on their ankle causing them to fall to the deck, sustaining a fractured elbow. The area was inspected and no defects were found. The passenger was medically disembarked.

Cruise ships passenger fractured ankle while playing pickleball. The guest stated that while they was playing pickleball on the sports court on the open deck, while trying to hit the ball, their right ankle twisted causing them to fall forward onto the deck.

A cruise ship passenger was playing table tennis when they tripped and fell, hitting a metal pole near the table. The passenger sustained a broken femur. The area was inspected and there were no defects found and the incident was thought to be loss of co-ordination/situational awareness on the part of the passenger.

A cruise ship passenger was playing table tennis when they fell backwards after trying to stretch for the ball. They landed on their left side and they tried to use their left arm to soften their landing, but sustained a fracture. They were wearing sports shoes at the time and the area did not have any defects. The injury was put down to carelessness.

A cruise ship passenger was on the sports court, they went to move and felt a pop on their ankle. The passenger went to the medical centre where a ruptured achilles tendon was diagnosed.  The area was inspected and found in a good condition; no defects or contributing factors noted. The guest will remain on board for the duration of the cruise.

representative_cases.description: A sailing vessel crew member fell, resulting in a broken leg. SAR assisted and injured person taken to hospital.
Sailing vessel crew member fell, injuring their ribs and subsequently broke two. The vessel made its own way to port and the casualty made their own way to hospital
Sailing charter vessel passenger fell and suffered a head injury. There was extensive bleeding from a wound on the head and fluid from the nose. The casualty was taken to hospital.
A sail training vessel crewmember was climbing down from their bunk in the dark when they slipped and fell. They sprained their ankle.
Sailing vessels crew member fell injuring head resulting in medivac.
A charter motor cruiser crew member slipped on deck resulting in crushed leg injury. Transferred to ambulance onto hospital.
Tall ship sailing vessel crewmember slipped on deck resulting in a head injury. The casualty was then taken by ambulance to hospital.
A sailing vessel crewmember slipped over in the cockpit and sustained a fractured ankle. They were taken off the vessel and via ambulance to for hospital treatment.
A broads cruiser crewmember fell onboard resulting in a 7.6cm laceration to their head. The crewmember was removed from the vessel and taken to hospital.
A sailing yacht crew member fell and sustained a head injury. The crew member was conscious and breathing but not fully alert with no superficial injury. The ambulance service was called to assist.

representative_cases.description: A ro-pax vessel lost it's starboard anchor and chain whilst in a bay waiting to enter dry dock. Harbour authorities were notified and a salvage team arranged to recover the anchor and chain.
A buoy laying vessel lost its port anchor. The place that the anchor was lost was marked so it could be retrieved later.
A cargo vessel dragged anchor, grounded afetr which it had difficulty starting its engines. Subsequently got underway and berthed alongside for damage assessement and repair prior to dry dock.
A bulk carrier drifted anchor passing within 1 cable of a container vessel. near miss
A cruise ship parted its anchor cable during heavy weather and started to drift. They recovered the remaining chain and then dropped the other anchor in a different position.
A tall ship dragged its anchor and was blown on to harbour breakwater. The two crew were successfully evacuated. The vessel was declared a total loss and broken up in situ .
A service vessel loss of starboard anchor and cable.
A cruise ship crew was weighing anchor and while bringing home starboard anchor, the shank parted. The lower portion of the shank and flukes were lost. There were no injuries to personnel.
Oil tanker collided with bulk carrier while at anchor resulting in damage to the bow.
A container vessel broke an anchor winch whilst heaving up at the anchorage.

representative_cases.description: Sailing vessel capsized with one person on board. The casualty was recovered from water with no sustained injuries. The vessel was towed back to safety.
A recreational motor vessel was capsized and swamped by a wave. It sank and the four crew on board were rescued and taken ashore. They were not injured.
Sailing yacht capsized with two crew on board. The crew were recovered by nearby vessels and the sailing yacht was towed back to shore. No injuries were reported.
RIB capsized with 4 people on board. The RIB was recovered by a local barge. Everyone safe and well, no damages reported.   All wearing lifejackets.  In water: 2 minutes.
A trimaran capsized with all three crew recovered safely to a search and rescue vessel. The vessel was left to drift as it could not be righted and towed, it was later found and towed to harbour.

Members of the public called the emergency services to report three sailing dinghies capsized and in difficulty. Search and rescue (SAR) assets were deployed to assist the sailors, three sailors were recovered by the sailing club safety boat and the other two recovered by the SAR vessel. The three dinghies were recovered and brought ashore. The five sailors were assessed by paramedics and then dis
A sailing vessel capsized. The vessel and all occupants were recovered to safety by a lifeboat. There were no reported injuries.
A speedboat capsized. No persons on board were injured and the services of search and rescue were not required.
A racing speedboat capsized whilst participating in a race. The crew were recovered, uninjured, and the vessel was towed to safety.
A sailing vessel capsized while racing with six crew onboard going into the water. All crew were recovered from the water and taken ashore. The vessel later sank.

representative_cases.description: A small recreational craft used for fishing capsized on a reservoir. The two crew entered the water, only one was wearing a lifejacket. They were in the water for around 3-4 minutes and then manged to climb onto the capsized vessel c and awaited assistance. The crew were safely recovered and brought ashore.

A recreational motor vessel with 4 occupants capsized. They were rescued by another recreational craft and the local lifeboat service also attended and escorted the other craft back to shore. The 4 occupants were given first aid at the lifeboat station and were allowed to leave after they were found not to be seriously injured. It was reported that the occupants of the boat had possibly consumed a
Two kayakers both capsized and entered the water. One kayaker self recovered while the other kayaker was not found and presumed deceased after an extensive rescue operation.

A sailing dinghy was found capsized with the helm reported missing. Search and rescue assets were tasked to the scene to carry out a search operation that was suspended overnight and then continued the following morning. The dinghy was found washed ashore but the helm was still missing presumed dead.

A dinghy capsized while the crew member was fishing. They were  sat on top of the upturned vessel. A passing by vessel noticed an individual waving and provided a lifejacket. The individual was helped and brought safely ashore.
Two persons were onboard a kayak when it capsized. One casualty was recovered but the other was declared deceased once found.

A dinghy crew member fell overboard from the vessel itself, who then managed to self recover themselves in the end with the police and lifeboats had been tasked to assist. One of the lifeboats also helped with dinghy recovery had a medical check to an there were no medical issues.

Man overboard from a dinghy clinging onto a buoy required assistance. The casualty is not a good swimmer and was not wearing a lifejacket. A passing yacht assisted the casualty who was taken ashore for further medical assistance.

A two person sit on kayak capsized on a white water section of the river with one paddler on board. The casualty became trapped under the kayak, the paddle and got their legs tangled in the webbing and leg leash leaving their head under the water. The victim was declared deceased by the doctor on the scene. They were wearing a personal flotation device at the time.
A kayaker capsized and was later found deceased.

description:

{
  "title": "Hatch Operation Injuries: Procedural and Design Failures",
  "description": "This theme encompasses crew injuries arising from the operation of shipboard hatches across a range of vessel types and operational contexts, including routine post-departure activity, emergency escape route inspections, and confined space egress. A consistent characteristic is that hatch handling is perceived as a low-risk, repetitive task, and consequently it is rarely subjected to formal risk assessment or documented safe work procedures. This organisational complacency creates a vulnerability that is readily exploited by situational variables — darkness, vessel motion in even moderate sea states, lone working, and poor equipment condition — converting what appears to be a trivial task into one with a credible injury pathway. The incidents range from finger-trap entrapments during routine hatch closing to falls from height caused by loss of control of heavy, unsupported hatch lids, and overbalancing due to incorrect body positioning, all of which share the common latent cause of inadequate hazard identification for a class of task that recurs throughout shipboard life.\n\nLatent hardware deficiencies play a significant enabling role across this cluster: hatches frequently lack finger-trap prevention features such as ergonomic handles or closing-assist mechanisms, and inspection hatches are found with stiff or corroded wheel locks, heavy lids with degraded hinge or counterbalance mechanisms, and no prop or stay rod to retain the hatch in the open position. These deficiencies increase the physical effort and postural awkwardness required to operate the equipment safely, raising the consequence of any lapse in technique or balance. Human factors compound this risk — inattention, instinctive reactions to prevent a fall, incorrect body positioning, and absence of supervision or a buddy system are recurring contributors. Environmental conditions, particularly night-time low-light environments and vessel motion from wave action, reduce both situational awareness and physical stability, tipping borderline situations into injury events. The combination of degraded hardware, absent procedural controls, and adverse environmental conditions represents a systemic failure in the management of foreseeable risk associated with routine hatch operation.",

title: Night-Time River Grounding Compounded by Tidal Flooding
description:
This theme is characterised by recreational and hire vessels grounding in shallow riverine or estuarial waters during night-time passages, with the grounding subsequently compounded into a serious flooding or sinking event by rising tidal conditions. The incidents share a consistent causal pathway: inadequate pre-departure voyage planning — particularly the failure to account for tidal states, depth margins, and the navigational demands of night-time river transit — creates the initial conditions for grounding. Once aground, crews demonstrate a further failure to anticipate or respond effectively to the dynamic tidal threat, neither kedging off promptly nor taking protective measures to prevent water ingress over low freeboard points such as the stern. The pattern recurs because these vessels are frequently operated by persons with limited underway passage-making experience, including houseboat operators and hire vessel users who are unaccustomed to applying systematic voyage planning disciplines to what may appear to be straightforward transits.

Latent hardware deficiencies — including insufficient or undersized bilge pumps, unsecured outboard engines, and damage-prone propulsion systems — reduce the vessel's capacity to self-recover once aground, transforming a recoverable grounding into a total loss. Human factors are dominant throughout: the absence of a formal watchkeeping regime, failure to use available depth-sounding equipment, and limited familiarity with local waterway hazards all reflect systemic shortfalls in safety culture and training among the recreational and hire sector. Environmental conditions, particularly reduced visibility at night and the unforgiving mechanics of tidal rise on a vessel displaced on a bank, act as force multipliers on these pre-existing vulnerabilities. The combination of an inexperienced crew, a vessel not optimally configured for passage-making, and an unplanned encounter with a tidal grounding scenario consistently exceeds the crew's capacity to manage the emergency effectively.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulations 34 and 35: safe navigation, voyage planning, and danger messages)
SOLAS Chapter III – Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (emergency preparedness, survival craft, and rescue procedures)
SOLAS Chapter II-1 – Construction: Subdivision, Stability, and Machinery (watertight integrity, bilge pumping arrangements, and damage survivability)
preventable_by: Mandate formal pre-departure voyage planning for all overnight or inter-port transits, requiring operators to document tidal predictions, minimum depth margins along the intended track, and contingency grounding response procedures — including kedging-off protocols and identification of safe waiting anchorages — before departure.

Require hire and recreational vessel operators navigating tidal rivers at night to maintain a continuous active watchkeeping regime, including regular depth-sounder monitoring with pre-set shallow-water alarms, and to complete a demonstrated competency assessment covering night navigation and tidal hazard management before undertaking such passages.

Establish minimum hardware standards for vessels transiting tidal rivers, including certified bilge pump capacity commensurate with vessel volume, secure engine mounting inspections prior to passage, and mandatory carriage of operational depth-sounding equipment — with enforcement through pre-departure vessel safety checks conducted by marina operators or hire companies.

representative_cases.description: A recreational sailing vessel was on passage in a river when it ran aground on a shoal. The vessel became stuck in the mud of the shoal and slipped down the bank so that the tide was able to come over the stern and flood the vessel, causing it to sink. Local SAR services extracted the two crew, who were unharmed, and marked the sunken vessel with a float until it could be recovered.

A motor cruiser grounded following an incident and became stuck on mud. The vessel was primarily a house boat but it was underway to another port where the crew had a second boat. The engine was damaged in the grounding. There were four people on board the boat as well as a large number of animals (fifteen dogs, eight cats and various other small animals including birds). The boat began to flood w

The crew members  were transferred to the SAR vessel, taken asho

Soon after a slight vibration was felt, it was suspected from the vessel briefly grounding on a possible new bank that may have developed. The crew continued with the exercise as the vessel seemed fine, after it was recovered there was damage to the port propell

A recreational vessel grounded and was listing about forty-five degrees, as the tide began to rise the vessel flooded through its vents as it was stuck hard aground. The two crewmembers were still onboard and moved to sit on the side of the vessel. Search and rescue services rescued the two crewmembers. The vessel then sank. There was 700 litres of fuel onboard the vessel and a sheen was on the wa

Support vessel grounded resulting in no known damage but loss of power and towage required to deeper waters. The vessel's propeller hit a small sandbank which cut out the engine. Several attempts were made to restart the engine but these failed. Another support vessel with a shallow draft went to the assistance of the support vessel, passed a tow line and towed the vessel into the deeper waters at

A RIB (rigid inflatable boat) with three people on board grounded on mud. The lifeboat extracted a child from the vessel whilst the two remaining people on board waited to refloat. As the vessel refloated, the vessel started to sink and the two people were rescued by the lifeboat.

A sailing yacht hit a sandbank resulting in the loss of one of the bilge keels, the vessel then capsized with the two crew onboard going into the water. Two search and rescue (SAR) vessels were launched, as they arrived the two crew were recovered from the water by a passing vessel. Both crew members were assessed by the SAR crew, no medical assistance was required. The vessel later sank with the

A search and rescue vessel was searching along the coast for a missing person when they went aground on a submerged rock shelf. The vessel was not significantly damaged and was able to continue the search for another hour before returning to their base, where the crew discovered that the propellers were damaged.

A sailing yacht ran aground and was unable to be towed due to the tidal height. The vessel later refloated on the flood tide and it was discovered the rudder had broken off. A search and rescue vessel was tasked to tow the vessel back to a mooring.

title: Undetected Flammable and Toxic Gas Accumulation Aboard Vessels
description:
This theme encompasses incidents arising from the undetected accumulation of toxic or flammable gases — principally carbon monoxide (CO) and liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) — within enclosed or semi-enclosed spaces aboard vessels. The incidents share a consistent causal architecture: a latent hardware deficiency, whether a compromised exhaust or combustion system, a leaking LPG tank, or a defective fuel system, allows dangerous gases to migrate or accumulate in occupied or unattended spaces. This hazard is compounded in every case by the absence or failure of automated detection systems, meaning that no independent safeguard exists to interrupt the progression toward a casualty event. The pattern recurs across vessel types — from small pleasure craft to commercial utility and crew transfer vessels — indicating that the underlying safety management failures are systemic rather than isolated. The asymmetry between severity outcomes, ranging from minor injury to vessel loss and animal fatality, reflects the degree to which chance, occupancy, and proximity to an ignition source determine consequences rather than any meaningful variation in underlying risk control.

Latent hardware deficiencies are the initiating condition in each incident, but the absence of procedural safeguards consistently transforms a manageable technical failure into a serious casualty. Crews and owners demonstrated no established pre-operation checks — such as gas sniff tests, bilge ventilation procedures, or exhaust system inspections — that would have intercepted accumulation before exposure or ignition occurred. Human factors, including failure to recognise early CO poisoning symptoms, the diminished alertness associated with night-time operation, and the reduced procedural rigour characteristic of single-handed or leisure vessel operation, further eroded the opportunity for timely intervention. Environmental conditions, notably low wind speeds and enclosed berthing arrangements, reduced natural ventilation effectiveness and accelerated accumulation rates. Collectively, these incidents demonstrate a well-documented failure mode in which the safety system has no depth: when the primary hardware barrier fails, no procedural, detection-based, or human-recognition layer exists to prevent harm.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-2: Construction — Fire Protection, Fire Detection and Fire Extinction
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code)
preventable_by: Mandate the installation, regular testing, and maintenance of type-approved CO and flammable gas detection systems with audible alarms in all accommodation spaces, galleys, and enclosed below-deck areas, with flag State or classification society verification at survey.

Require vessel operators to incorporate pre-departure and pre-operation atmospheric safety checks — including LPG system integrity inspections, bilge ventilation confirmation, and exhaust system condition assessments — into formal Safety Management System procedures, with completion records retained aboard.

Deliver targeted crew and owner safety training on the recognition of early CO poisoning symptoms, LPG accumulation risks, and the correct emergency response to gas alarm activation, with competency in enclosed-space atmospheric hazards integrated into relevant certification and familiarisation requirements.

The shore side emergency services responded and took both casualties to hospital in an ambulance. title: Passenger Bathroom Slip Hazards in Marine Environments
description:
This theme encompasses a recurring pattern of passenger slip, trip, and fall incidents within cruise ship cabin bathrooms, predominantly occurring in shower areas and at shower exit points. The incidents are characterised by wet floor surfaces, inadequate or absent fixed anti-slip provisions, and unsecured or bypassed bathmats in a confined, dynamic marine environment. A common thread across all incidents is the absence of effective passive safety infrastructure — specifically fixed non-slip floor treatments and appropriately positioned grab rails — combined with an over-reliance on moveable soft furnishings such as towel bathmats as the primary, and in many cases sole, slip-prevention measure. These incidents recur not because of isolated acts of passenger carelessness, but because the vessel's safety management system has failed to recognise unsecured bathmats and unprotected wet floor areas as foreseeable, systemic hazards requiring active mitigation in the marine cabin environment.

Latent hardware deficiencies are central to this theme: bathroom design frequently lacks fixed anti-slip surface treatments, secondary grab rails at shower exit points, and appropriately positioned support structures for passengers with mobility limitations. Human factors compound these deficiencies, as passengers — unfamiliar with vessel motion and without adequate safety briefings or cabin literature — make uninformed decisions such as foregoing available bathmats or showering without adequate support. Environmental conditions, including sea states ranging from slight to moderate and night-time operation, further elevate risk by introducing vessel motion and reducing passenger situational awareness in an already hazardous wet space. The post-incident finding of no defects in multiple cases reveals a systemic failure in hazard identification: the vessel's defect reporting and safety management frameworks do not adequately classify soft furnishing placement, fixed anti-slip provision, or grab rail adequacy as auditable safety-critical items, allowing the latent risk to persist undetected and unaddressed across the fleet.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction — Subdivision, Stability, and Machinery (structural arrangements and design safety of passenger spaces)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (passenger safety briefings and muster procedures, including onboard safety information obligations)
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code — requirement for operators to identify hazards, assess risks, and implement effective safety management procedures)
preventable_by: Mandate the installation of fixed, permanent anti-slip surface treatments on all passenger bathroom and shower floor areas, and the fitting of at least one securely mounted grab rail at each shower entry and exit point, with compliance verified through flag state or class surveys and documented in the vessel's maintenance management system.

Establish a formal housekeeping and cabin safety protocol requiring crew to inspect and confirm that bathmats have adequate non-slip backing and are correctly positioned at each cabin turnaround, with findings recorded in a structured checklist that feeds into the vessel's hazard identification and defect reporting framework under the ISM-compliant Safety Management System.

Implement mandatory passenger safety communications — delivered at embarkation briefings, through in-cabin video, and printed cabin literature — that specifically address the risk of slipping in cabin bathrooms during vessel motion, the correct use of provided anti-slip aids, and the importance of reporting bathroom hazards or injuries to the medical centre without delay.

The shower curtain in subject and the area of the accident were inspected and revealed no defects. When the vessel came alongside, five d

The floor was slippery due to the water and the passenger slipped and fell, stretching out their right leg causing a strain which resulted in the rupture of the Achilles tendon on the right leg. There were no defects found in the cabin and the full lighting

The onboard emergency call was activated and the guest was taken to the medical centre for treatment. The area where the accident occurred was examined and the shower base was found in good condition without any obvious defect. The occurrence was reported to

A cruise ship passenger was exiting their cabin bathroom when they slipped on the floor and fell forward, landing on their left side. They sustained a fractured pelvis. The area was inspected and the floor was dry at the time with no defects noted.

As the passenger stepped into the toilets, they slipped on the wet floor with one leg slipping in front and the other going underneath them. One of the medical team was called for and assisted the passenger. The passenger was medically disembarked and taken to a local hospital and treated for a fractured ankle. The passenger

The incident happened in the evening and they went to the medical centre on board the day after. The area was inspected for defects and none were found. The guest didn't immediately go to the medical centre on board but attended later on two days after the injury. The area was inspected and found to be in order without defects. The passenger fractured their shoulder.

title: Night-Time Contact with Submerged and Fixed Hazards
description:
This theme is characterised by vessel contact with submerged objects, fixed infrastructure, and navigational hazards during night-time operations, predominantly in coastal and inshore environments. The incidents span a range of vessel types — including SAR vessels, ferries, passenger vessels, and recreational craft — but share a consistent pattern: darkness as the primary environmental risk amplifier, combined with inadequate hazard awareness, insufficient pre-passage planning, and reduced lookout effectiveness. The recurrence of these incidents across operationally experienced crews underscores that professional competence alone does not mitigate the structural vulnerabilities introduced by night operations. Compounding factors include poorly maintained or unmarked aids to navigation, decommissioned or inadequately charted infrastructure remaining in navigable waters, and the operational urgency characteristic of emergency response missions, which may suppress standard navigational caution. Familiarity with a route or launch area further contributes to complacency, reducing crew vigilance around non-obvious but persistent hazards.

From a latent causation perspective, these incidents reflect the convergence of hardware deficiencies, human factors, and environmental conditions. Hardware and infrastructure failures — including aging power plants, dislodged transom shields, submerged mooring blocks, and poorly maintained buoys — create contact hazards that are difficult to detect without adequate lighting or depth-monitoring equipment. Human factors include over-reliance on experiential knowledge rather than systematic passage planning, insufficient use of available navigational instruments such as echo sounders and chart plotters, and degraded watchkeeping discipline during routine or return transits. Environmental conditions, particularly darkness, interact with these latent failures to eliminate the visual margins that would otherwise allow crews to identify and avoid hazards in time. Collectively, these incidents indicate systemic gaps in night navigation protocols, hazard charting accuracy, and the maintenance standards applied to both vessels and navigational infrastructure.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter V – Safety of Navigation (Regulations 19, 22, 24, and 34: navigational equipment requirements, lookout duties, safe speed, and voyage planning)
SOLAS Chapter IV – Radiocommunications (in the context of coordination failures and emergency communication during contact events)
SOLAS Chapter IX – Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code: safety management systems, passage planning procedures, and crew training obligations)
preventable_by: Mandate structured night navigation protocols for all vessel types operating in coastal and inshore waters, requiring documented pre-departure passage plans that include hazard identification, speed restrictions in hazard-prone areas, and confirmation of functional depth-monitoring and chart-plotting equipment prior to each night transit.

Establish a systematic programme for hazard mapping and nautical chart updating in frequently used departure routes and operational areas, including the recording and formal reporting of all submerged obstructions, decommissioned infrastructure, and displaced or damaged aids to navigation to the relevant hydrographic and port authorities.

Implement mandatory crew training in night navigation and loss-of-power emergency response scenarios, with periodic drills that simulate contact risk conditions, reinforcing lookout discipline, speed management under reduced visibility, and the procedural steps required when propulsion or power loss occurs in proximity to fixed or submerged hazards.

representative_cases.description: A ferry experienced power failure and struck a disused mooring dolphin. There was no damage to the vessel but possibly damage to the dolphin.
A search and rescue vessel was on passage when it made contact with a submerged buoy. No damage or injuries occurred.
A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was underway when the skeg of the vessel struck a rock. The vessel was not stuck but the skeg was damaged.
Passenger vessel hit an unknown object underwater which resulted in loss of propulsion. Towage was required. Following examination - transom shield had become dislodged. No further damages/injuries.
Lifeboat was tasking when it struck something under the water - possibly a rock. The only damage was to the gelcoat on the port side hull. No injuries reported.
A lifeboat damaged its propeller after passing through a narrow passage when the propeller made contact with a submerged rock.
Whilst returning to port the vessel's prop made contact with a submerged object, suspected to be a rock. The vessel returned to station and the propeller changed.  There were no injuries to crew.
A sailing vessel made contact with a submarine barrier. The iron keel was potentially hit however no damage was confirmed and it remained on passage to its final destination.
Chartered yacht sank on its moorings after being struck by a SAR tug. All persons onboard the yacht escaped unhurt.
A sailing vessel became ensnared in an underwater obstruction that fouled the propeller. Divers attended and freed the vessel and it was able to continue on passage without further issues.

A tug was assisting a container ship when a tow parted. No injuries, no damages and gear was recovered. Both of them continued their passage.

Tug unsuitable to provide assistance due to deficient winch.
A tug ran over its own towline and had damages including a bent shaft, arriving at the port to undergo repairs.
An aft tug's tow line parted after winch failure while berthing a container ship. Berthing operations were completed successfully using other tugs involved in the operation.

title: Engine Room Fire from Degraded Components and Inspection Failures
description:
This theme is characterised by engine room fires arising from the convergence of degraded or defective hardware — including contaminated thermal lagging, failed fuel system fittings, circlip failures, and oil leaks near high-temperature turbocharger surfaces — and systemic failures in maintenance and pre-departure inspection regimes. Across these incidents, a consistent pattern emerges: a latent mechanical deficiency develops undetected through the maintenance and repair cycle, only to manifest as a fire once the engine is started and heat builds over time. The recurring nature of these events indicates that existing inspection protocols are insufficiently rigorous in targeting the interface between potential hydrocarbon release points and ignition-capable hot surfaces, and that pre-departure readiness checks do not consistently include verification of thermal insulation integrity and fuel system fitting condition.

Latent hardware deficiencies — fatigue-failed circlips, degraded valve plugs, deteriorated lagging saturated with hydrocarbons, and seeping turbocharger seals — form the foundational risk in each case, but their escalation to fire is enabled by human and procedural failures at multiple barriers. Maintenance personnel do not identify or rectify degraded components during repair periods, and pre-departure checks do not capture residual risk before engine start-up. Once underway, the absence of automated oil mist or fuel leak detection systems means the crew's first indication of a developing fault is often the fire itself rather than a precursor warning. Environmental factors, particularly night-time operations reducing visual acuity in the engine room and moderate sea states inducing mechanical vibration on already-stressed fittings, further compress the available window for human detection and intervention, transforming what should be a maintainable defect into a serious fire casualty.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-2: Construction – Fire Protection, Fire Detection and Fire Extinction
SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Structure, Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations
SOLAS Chapter IX: Management for the Safe Operation of Ships (ISM Code)
preventable_by: Mandate structured pre-departure engine room technical inspections following any repair or maintenance period, with a dedicated checklist item requiring physical verification of the condition, integrity, and contamination status of all thermal lagging and insulation on or adjacent to main engines and turbochargers, signed off by a responsible engineer before engine start-up.

Require the installation and routine testing of automated oil mist detection and hydrocarbon/fuel vapour detection systems in engine rooms, particularly in the vicinity of high-temperature components such as turbochargers and exhaust manifolds, so that developing leaks are alarmed at source before ignition can occur rather than being discovered only after a fire has started.

Introduce mandatory, timed engine room watchkeeping rounds during all machinery run-in and warm-up phases — including those conducted at night or in port — with minimum inspection intervals not exceeding 30 minutes, documented in the engine room log, to ensure that early indicators of fire such as smoke, unusual odour, or surface discolouration are detected and acted upon before a fire becomes established.

representative_cases.description: A trailing suction hopper dredger was alongside after a repair period. Departure was expected for 0800 on the day of the incident, so at 0420 the main engine was started to run it in preparation. At 0630, an engineer noticed smoke rising from the main engine heat shields. The crew mustered and fire fighting measures were implemented. After the fire was extinguished it was confirmed that there had

A crew transfer vessel was underway when a fault alarm sounded on one of the engines followed by a fire alarm. The crew saw flames on the CCTV system. They shut down the fuel supply and ventilation for the engine and alerted the coastguard. They then released the fixed fire fighting system (STAT-X). The vessel made its way back to port with an escort from another vessel. The fire was reportedly ca

A pilot vessel had a fire in the engine room which was extinguished by crew using a dry powder extinguisher.  It was found that the main engine turbo lagging had caught fire after a suspected oil leak.

A ro-pax vessel had departed port when a high exhaust temperature alarm sounded, upon investigating it was found that the fuel return pipeline’s circlip had sheared off and started to leak fuel over the hot engine. The bridge was contacted and requested to slow down and stop the aft main engine. The vessel was able to proceed safely to port using the forward main engine. With assistance from a tug

Hopper dredger suffered a fire as a result of a failed fuel injector and manifold gasket. The flames were approximately 2-3 inches high and the fire was immediately extinguished using the nearest foam portable fire extinguisher. No injuries were sustained on board. The injector and manifold gasket were both replaced.

Whilst a tug was pushing up on a vessel, fire alarms were set off in the engine room. The crew were mustered investigated. A fire was reported on the starboard engine. Emergency shutdown on the starboard engine was initiated immediately. Fuel came into contact with a hot surface and ignited. The fire was started because the lay of a low-pressure fuel hose was rubbing on a corner of the engine cove

A ro-pax vessel was in passage when one of the crew members noticed smoke above the starboard main engine. The crew member raised the alarm and the crew mustered. Whilst a two person fire fighting team wearing breathing apparatus was preparing, another crew member took immediate action using a CO2 fire extinguisher. They noticed the flames were on the lagging of the exhaust and this initial action

A ro-ro passenger vessel had a fire in the lagging over the starboard main engine while making approach to harbour. The fire was extinguished by a portable fire extinguisher within four minutes of being reported. The cause of the fire has been identified as a joint on the lube oil sensor pipework which had worked lose and sprayed oil onto the hot engine.

A pilot vessel had a fire in the engine room after a lube oil pipe failed and sprayed oil onto a hot surface. The vessel crew initiated fire fighting activities and activated the fixed fire fighting system and supressed the fire. The vessel then made its way back to berth under tow from another pilot vessel, where they were met by the shore side fire service who checked over the vessel. There were

An inland waterways passenger vessel was underway approaching the berth when there was a fuel pressure alarm on the port engine. After investigating, the crew discovered that fuel was leaking from the main engine and there was white smoke coming out of the engine room ventilation followed by the fire alarm being activated. The crew carried out emergency actions to shut down the engine and fuel sup

title: Bilge System Failure and Inadequate Crew Familiarisation
description:
This theme is characterised by flooding emergencies in which vessels' primary dewatering systems fail at the critical moment they are needed, transforming manageable water ingress into life-safety emergencies requiring external rescue intervention. Across the representative incidents, a consistent pattern emerges: the bilge pump — whether rendered inoperable by mechanical failure, electrical power loss, or lack of maintenance — constitutes a single point of failure with no redundant backup. Compounding this, crew are frequently unfamiliar with vessel-specific systems, particularly in bareboat charter contexts, delaying identification of the ingress source and the isolation of contributing valves or fittings. The recurring outcome — vessels requiring SAR assistance or towing to safety — indicates that these incidents are not isolated equipment failures but systemic deficiencies in vessel readiness and crew preparedness.

Latent hardware deficiencies are the dominant enabling condition in this theme, manifesting as unmaintained bilge pumps, fractured pump handles, failed electrical systems, and the absence of independently powered or manual backup dewatering capacity. Human factors intersect critically with these hardware failures: inadequate pre-departure checks allow non-functional equipment to go undetected, while the absence of structured handover processes in charter arrangements leaves crew without the system knowledge necessary to respond effectively under pressure. Environmental conditions — particularly adverse sea states and night-time operations — consistently amplify the consequences by accelerating water ingress beyond manageable levels and reducing the crew's situational awareness and response effectiveness. Together, these latent conditions align to convert foreseeable, preventable equipment states into active flooding emergencies.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-1: Construction – Subdivision, Stability, Machinery and Electrical Installations (bilge pumping arrangements and watertight integrity requirements)
SOLAS Chapter III: Life-Saving Appliances and Arrangements (emergency equipment readiness and crew familiarity with safety systems)
SOLAS Chapter V: Safety of Navigation (pre-departure safety checks, voyage planning, and fitness of vessel for intended service)
preventable_by: Mandate pre-departure safety checklists that explicitly verify the operational status of all bilge pumps, including test activation, and require documented confirmation that at least one independently powered or manual backup bilge pump is aboard and functional before the vessel is permitted to depart.

Require that all bareboat charter operators provide a documented, vessel-specific familiarisation briefing to charterers prior to departure, covering the location, function, and operation of all critical safety systems including sea cocks, bilge pumps, and fire suppression valves, with sign-off retained as a condition of charter.

Introduce mandatory carriage requirements for small recreational and charter vessels to carry a secondary bilge pump with independent power (battery-isolated electric or manual hand pump with a rated spare handle), ensuring dewatering capability is maintained in the event of primary pump failure or total electrical power loss.

representative_cases.description: A workboat began to experience water ingress. The crewmember on board tried to identify the source but could not. They tried to operate the hand bilge pump but the handle snapped. They proceeded to take the vessel to a slipway where they turned off the engine and went back down to the engine room to observe the water intake. They turned off the main seacock valve but the water was still coming in.

A motor cruiser with two crew on board suffered electrical power failure and subsequently experienced water ingress that the bilge pump on board could not keep up with. A search and rescue vessel was dispatched that provided a pump to get the water ingress under control and then towed the vessel to a harbour. There were no injuries or pollution.

A recreational motor vessel started taking on water and experienced engine failure. The pump was not functioning and the two crew were bailing the water but unable to control the level of ingress.  The vessel was towed to safety by a search and rescue (SAR) vessel.

A recreational motor vessel started taking on water resulting in power failure. The water ingress could not be identified, crew onboard stated bailing out the water as the pump was not working. Two search and rescue (SAR) vessels were tasked to assist. Five crew were recovered to one of the SAR vessels with the remaining two crew staying aboard to continue bailing. The vessel was towed back to sho

A fishing vessel experienced engine failure and started taking on water, the vessel's bilge pumps were managing to pump out the water. The vessel was anchored with five crew and twelve passengers onboard. A search and rescue vessel attended to assist if required. The vessel required towing to a harbour. No pollution was reported.

A fishing vessel had water ingress through the stern gland and the bilge pumps could not be operated effectively due to the skipper being new to the vessel. The crewmember panicked distracting the skipper from trying to work out how to control the pumps through a valve chest and the vessel was beginning to fill with water. A SAR (search and rescue) helicopter and lifeboat attended on scene and the

Sailing yacht with five people on board called MAYDAY after the vessel started filling with water. Search and rescue assets were tasked to the vessel but it was soon discovered that the cause of the water ingress was the bilge pump which was pumping water into the vessel rather than out.

A vessel was taking on water. A lifeboat and a police launch were on scene and they requested the vessel's crewmembers to stop their engines which appeared to help with stopping more water ingress. The bilge pumps were successful onboard which ensured the water ingress was completely stopped . The vessel was towed by a rescue vessel into a marina.

A sailing yacht suffered engine failure and started taking on water. The two crew anchored the vessel and started to bail by hand as the bilge pump was not working. The water ingress was located to a hairline crack approximately 4 metres on the starboard side by the starboard keel. The skipper phoned (as the vessel did not have a VHF) for assistance but did not want a search and rescue (SAR) vesse

A recreational motor vessel suffered water ingress, the bilge pump onboard was not working. The crew onboard started bailing the water out but were unable to manage the ingress. A search and rescue (SAR) vessel was tasked to assist. The water ingress was coming in through the prop shaft, the SAR vessel towed the motor vessel to a marina where the vessel was lifted out. Both crew onboard were weari

title: Exclusion Zone Failures During Cargo Handling Operations
description: Latent hardware deficiencies — including the absence of tag lines for suspended load control, missing banksmen equipment, and unsecured loads on forklifts — removed the last physical safeguards against injury once human and environmental factors aligned adversely. Human factors, particularly complacency toward habitual tasks, inadequate situational awareness (compounded in several cases by carrying loads that obstructed sightlines), and operational pressure to sustain cargo schedules, consistently overrode risk perception. Environmental conditions — night-time lighting, moderate sea states inducing vessel motion, and wind speeds sufficient to cause pallet swing — acted as force multipliers on already degraded procedural controls. The convergence of these latent conditions means that injury was structurally embedded in the way these operations were being conducted, and only systemic intervention at the procedural and supervisory level will interrupt the pattern.
solas_chapters: SOLAS Chapter II-2 — Construction: Fire protection, detection and extinction (as applicable to safe working space management and egress route integrity)
SOLAS Chapter III — Life-saving appliances and arrangements (crew safety during embarkation/disembarkation equipment operations including accommodation ladders)
SOLAS Chapter VI — Carriage of cargoes and oil fuels (safe handling, loading, and securing of cargo including forklift and crane operations)
preventable_by: Mandate and physically enforce load exclusion zones during all lifting operations by deploying a designated banksman with authority to halt operations, using physical barriers (cones, barriers, or rope lines) to demarcate drop/swing zones, and requiring pre-lift toolbox talks that explicitly assign crew positions away from the hazard area before any load is raised.

Require tag line use as a non-negotiable standard for all suspended pallet and cargo lifts conducted in conditions where wind, vessel motion, or load geometry creates lateral movement risk, with tag line deployment verified by the officer in charge prior to lift commencement and recorded on the cargo operation checklist.

Implement a formal traffic management plan for all forklift and vehicle operations in shared pedestrian spaces — including ramps, weather decks, and provisions areas — specifying segregated access routes, mandatory banksman deployment for reversing or inclined-surface operations, and a housekeeping standard that prohibits placement of pallets, trestles, or equipment within designated egress corridors, enforced through pre-operation area inspections.

representative_cases.description: During cargo loading, 2 crew members were injured by a pallet that swung in the wind. The first crew member to be injured was crushed between the swinging pallet and a pallet that had already been loading. The second crew member was injured because the swinging pallet fell onto his foot. Both went to hospital as a precautionary measure. Neither sustained serious injuries.

Cruise ship's crew member was working inside the deck port side frozen meat room. While coming out carrying one meat bag on their left shoulder they did not notice a group of stacked steel pallets and accidently hit their left leg against the corner of a pallet sustaining a left leg abrasion. They requested medical care and were declared unfit for duty.

A ro-ro cargo vessel crew member was reversing a forklift down the fixed internal ramp between weather deck and main deck transferring a trestle. They noticed a crew member walking up the ramp. The crew member in the forklift adjusted the steering to give more clearance and applied the brakes to reduce speed further. At this point the fork lift sheared to the to the left then toppled over. The tre
Container ship's crew member suffered a hip and leg fracture after being crushed in between containers. The three containers were locked together and swung trapping the crew member.

A container ship crewmember was injured while trying to stow the ship's accommodation ladder. While standing on the quay, a shore-side forklift was assisting in lifting the bottom of the accommodation ladder clear of an obstruction when it became snagged. The crewmember was trying to see what was snagging the ladder when it broke free and hit them in the jaw. They were taken to hospital and underw

A crew transfer vessel was carrying out a lifting operation whilst alongside. A crew member was struck in the head by either the load or the strop bearing the load. They sustained a concussion and were advised to take 4 to 6 days off work.

A ro-pax vessel crew was loading stores from a lorry that was parked on board in the vehicle decks. The stores were in wheeled trollies that the crew used to move the stores to and from the lorry. The lorry was fitted with a rear lifting platform and whilst loading the empty trollies back onto the lorry, the driver pulled one of the trollies causing another trolley to roll off the lifting platform
A gangway dropped on crewmember's head while being craned ashore using ship's gear. They were taken to hospital for an operation.

A lifeboat crewmember was stowing a warp and chain from deck into stowage below deck through a spurling pipe. The rope fouled with a shackle coming through. As the chain was being guided down, the shackle fell from an upright position on to casualty's head.

A stevedore was undertaking discharge operations in the vessel's hold where one pallet of MDF (Medium Density Fibreboard) had been lifted by a forklift for rigging the sling and the MDF ended up sliding down the forklift forks. The MDF fell down and hit them on their leg.

Night-time operations further compound these deficiencies by reducing the crew's ability to monitor towline tension, detect early signs of wear or overloading, and manage tow geometry effectively. Due to deteriorating weather conditions the tow line broke three times on the passage, a tug took over the tow to bring the fishing vessel alongside. No reported crew injuries.

Environmental conditions, particularly night-time operations in sea states 3 and 4, consistently feature across incidents, reducing both visual awareness and physical stability.
